standards.standard_description: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child to point to the exact sentence that answers a question about the story.

questions.prompt:
Captain Rosie sailed her tiny boat across the lake every morning. One foggy day, she spotted something glowing beneath the water. She leaned over the side and saw a golden chest resting on the sandy bottom.

What did Captain Rosie find under the water?
questions.hint: Look at the last sentence — what was resting on the sandy bottom?

questions.prompt:
Marcus had practiced his speech twenty times. When he stepped onto the stage, his hands were shaking. But once he started talking, the words flowed easily, and the audience clapped loudly at the end.

How did Marcus feel when he first stepped on stage?
questions.hint: The text says his hands were shaking — what feeling does that show?

questions.prompt:
Grandma Chen kept a garden full of herbs. "Every plant has a story," she told Mei. She pointed to the lavender. "Your grandfather brought those seeds from his village."

Who brought the lavender seeds?
questions.hint: Grandma says 'Your grandfather brought those seeds.' Whose grandfather?

questions.prompt:
The fox crept through the snow, its orange fur bright against the white. It paused, tilted its head, then leaped straight up and dove nose-first into a snowbank. Seconds later, it emerged with a mouse in its jaws.

Why did the fox leap into the snowbank?
questions.hint: What did the fox have when it came back out of the snow?

questions.prompt:
Aisha's violin had belonged to three generations of her family. The wood was worn smooth where countless fingers had pressed the strings. "Play with your heart, not just your hands," her mother always said.

What does the worn wood tell us about the violin?
choices: It's been played by many people over many years
questions.correct: It's been played by many people over many years
questions.hint: The text says it belonged to three generations and has smooth, worn wood from countless fingers.

questions.prompt:
Deshawn saw the last cookie on the plate and reached for it. Then he noticed his little sister Mira coloring quietly in the corner, still wearing her soccer uniform. He remembered she had scored the winning goal that afternoon but nobody had celebrated because Grandma was sick. Deshawn put the cookie on a napkin, walked over, and set it beside Mira's crayons without saying a word. She looked up and smiled.

Why did Deshawn give Mira the cookie?
choices: He wanted to do something kind for Mira because her big moment had been overlooked
He wanted Mira to stop coloring and play soccer with him outside
He was not really hungry and did not want the cookie for himself
He was afraid Mira would tell their parents that he took the last cookie
questions.correct: He wanted to do something kind for Mira because her big moment had been overlooked
questions.hint: Look at the two things Deshawn remembered right before he gave up the cookie. Put them together to figure out how he was feeling about Mira.

questions.prompt:
Every recess, Priya sat alone on the bench and read her book. The other kids thought she was shy. But Priya always sat where she could see the whole playground, and she kept a small notebook tucked inside her book. When Coach asked who had seen who knocked over the equipment shed, only Priya raised her hand.

What does the text suggest about Priya?
choices: She is not telling the truth about what she saw
She notices and pays attention to what happens around her
She is too shy to make any friends at recess
She would rather read than play with the other kids
questions.correct: She notices and pays attention to what happens around her
questions.hint: The other kids have one idea about Priya, but the details about where she sits and what she keeps hidden point to something different. Trust the details over the opinion.

questions.prompt:
The morning of the trip, Leo checked the sky three times before breakfast. He packed his raincoat even though the forecast said sunny. He added an extra pair of dry socks, a flashlight, and a whistle to his bag. His mom laughed and said, 'It's just a walk in the park.' Leo zipped the bag anyway.

Which word BEST describes Leo, based on the text?
questions.hint: Do not pick the word just because it sounds a little like how Leo acts. Look at ALL the things he packed and ask what kind of person prepares like that.

questions.prompt:
Nadia and her older brother Theo were building a sandcastle. Theo kept saying, 'Let me do the towers, you're too little.' Every time Nadia patted a tower into shape, Theo smoothed it over and did it his own way. When their dad came to look, he said, 'Wonderful towers, Theo!' Theo grinned. Nadia stood up, brushed the sand off her knees, and went to sit by the water alone.

How does Nadia most likely feel at the end?
choices: Tired, because building the sandcastle took a very long time
Excited, because she finished building the sandcastle by herself
Angry at her dad for not letting them keep building the castle
Left out, because her work was taken over and only Theo was praised
questions.correct: Left out, because her work was taken over and only Theo was praised
questions.hint: Nadia does not say how she feels. Look at what happened to her towers and who got the praise, then think about why she walked away by herself.

questions.prompt:
For weeks, the old lighthouse keeper had left a bowl of milk on the porch each night, and each morning it was empty. He told the villagers a friendly ghost drank it. One night he stayed up and watched from the window. In the moonlight, a thin gray cat crept onto the porch and lapped up every drop. The next evening, the keeper set out the milk as usual and smiled to himself, but he never told the villagers what he had seen.

Why does the keeper keep putting out the milk AND stay quiet about the cat?
choices: He enjoys taking care of the cat and likes keeping the fun story alive for the villagers
He is worried the villagers will make him get rid of the stray cat
He still believes a ghost might be drinking some of the milk too
He forgot to tell the villagers and plans to explain it to them soon
questions.correct: He enjoys taking care of the cat and likes keeping the fun story alive for the villagers
questions.hint: He now KNOWS the truth, yet he does two things anyway: keeps feeding it and keeps the secret. Ask what both of those choices, plus his smile, tell you about what he wants.

questions.prompt: Maya packed her red backpack for the hike. She put in water, a map, and a snack. Which color was Maya's backpack?
questions.hint: Look for the word right before "backpack" in the story.

questions.prompt: Ben's dog Rex loves to run in the park. Every Saturday, Ben takes Rex there to play fetch. Where does Ben take Rex on Saturdays?
questions.hint: Find the place named in the story where Ben and Rex go.

questions.prompt: The class grew a bean plant on the windowsill. It grew tall because it got plenty of sunlight and water. Why did the bean plant grow tall?
choices: Because it got sunlight and water
questions.correct: Because it got sunlight and water
questions.hint: The story tells you two things the plant got. That is your reason.

questions.prompt: Lily felt happy when she saw the birthday cake with her name on it. How did Lily feel?
questions.hint: The story uses a feeling word to describe Lily. Look for it.

questions.prompt: During the storm, the family stayed inside and read books by candlelight. What did the family do during the storm?
questions.hint: The story says exactly what the family did. Find that part.

standards.standard_description: Recount stories including fables, folktales, and myths; determine the central message, lesson, or moral.
standards.parent_tip: After reading fables or folktales, ask 'What lesson was the author trying to teach?'

questions.prompt:
The crow found a piece of cheese. A fox said, "What a beautiful voice you must have! Please sing for me." The crow opened its beak to sing, and the cheese fell right into the fox's mouth.

What is the moral of this fable?
choices: Don't trust flattery from someone who wants something
questions.correct: Don't trust flattery from someone who wants something
questions.hint: The fox used fake compliments to trick the crow. What's the lesson?

questions.prompt:
A boy cried "Wolf!" when there was no wolf, just to get attention. The villagers came running twice. When a real wolf appeared and the boy cried for help, no one believed him.

What is the central message?
questions.hint: What happened because the boy lied? Nobody believed him when it mattered.

questions.prompt:
King Midas wished that everything he touched would turn to gold. His wish came true! But when his food, his flowers, and even his daughter turned to gold, he begged for the wish to be taken away.

What lesson does this myth teach?
choices: Gold is the most valuable thing
Be careful what you wish for — greed has consequences
questions.correct: Be careful what you wish for — greed has consequences
questions.hint: Midas got exactly what he wanted, but it ruined everything he loved.

questions.prompt:
Two goats met on a narrow bridge over a river. Neither would step aside. They pushed and shoved until both fell into the water below.

What is the lesson?
choices: Stubbornness and refusing to compromise hurts everyone
questions.correct: Stubbornness and refusing to compromise hurts everyone
questions.hint: Neither goat would give way. What happened because of that?

questions.prompt:
A young girl named Anansi's daughter was always bragging about being the smartest in her village. One day, the village elder gave her a riddle she couldn't solve. A quiet boy named Kofi, who listened more than he talked, solved it easily.

What is the central message?
choices: Girls aren't as smart as boys
Bragging doesn't make you smart — listening and thinking do
questions.correct: Bragging doesn't make you smart — listening and thinking do
questions.hint: Compare the girl who bragged with the boy who listened. Who succeeded?

questions.prompt:
All summer, Mole dug and stacked tunnels while the other meadow animals laughed. "Why work on such a sunny day?" they teased, and went back to playing. When the cold rains came and flooded the meadow, the animals had nowhere dry to hide. Then Mole opened his warm tunnels and let everyone crowd inside.

What is the central lesson of this story?
choices: Getting ready ahead of time pays off when hard times come
Rain can flood a meadow very quickly
You should always share your home with others
Moles are better at digging than other animals
questions.correct: Getting ready ahead of time pays off when hard times come
questions.hint: Ask what Mole did all summer that the others did not, and why it mattered later. The main lesson explains the whole story, not just the last line.

questions.prompt:
The proud peacock begged the eagle, "Teach me to fly high into the clouds!" The eagle answered, "Your feathers are too heavy for the sky." The peacock spread his glowing tail and cried, "But no bird is as beautiful as I am!" Then the eagle soared up and disappeared into the clouds, while the peacock stayed on the ground, staring at his own reflection in a pond.

What lesson does the story teach?
choices: Peacocks would rather stay near ponds than fly
What you can do matters more than how you look
Looking beautiful is more important than flying
Eagles are unkind to birds that ask for help
questions.correct: What you can do matters more than how you look
questions.hint: Notice which bird actually reached the sky. Be careful — the peacock's own words tell you what HE believes, not what the story is teaching.

questions.prompt:
A tired traveler asked an old woman, "How far is it to the town?" "Just keep walking," she said. Annoyed, he snapped, "Give me a real answer!" The woman said nothing. She watched him take a few steps down the road, then called out, "Two hours." "Why didn't you say so before?" he demanded. "Because," she said, "first I had to see how fast you walk."

What is the main message of this story?
choices: It is rude to demand answers from strangers
Older people always give the wisest advice
A good answer sometimes needs information first
Some questions simply cannot be answered
questions.correct: A good answer sometimes needs information first
questions.hint: Focus on the woman's very last sentence — it explains WHY she waited before answering. The lesson lives in her reason.

questions.prompt:
Two frogs tumbled into a deep bucket of cream. One frog looked at the slippery walls and said, "This is hopeless," then stopped moving and sank. The other frog kicked and kicked all night, refusing to quit. By morning his kicking had churned the cream into a lump of solid butter, and he hopped right out to safety.

What lesson does this fable teach?
choices: Cream will turn into butter if it is stirred enough
When trouble is too big, it is safer to stay still
Some problems really are impossible to escape
Not giving up can turn a hopeless problem into a solution
questions.correct: Not giving up can turn a hopeless problem into a solution
questions.hint: Compare the two frogs and what happened to each. The lesson comes from the frog who lived, not the one who quit.

questions.prompt:
A little stream begged the great river, "Let me rush ahead and reach the sea today, like you!" The river said, "Slow down. The sea will still be there." But the stream raced on, splashing wildly over the rocks. It spread out thin across the hot, dry sand and soaked away long before it ever reached the sea. The river flowed on, calm and steady, and arrived that same evening.

What is the central message of this story?
choices: Being steady and patient reaches a goal better than rushing
Streams should never try to flow toward the sea
Bigger and stronger things will always win in the end
Water will always soak away into dry sand
questions.correct: Being steady and patient reaches a goal better than rushing
questions.hint: The stream and river wanted the same thing but ended up differently. Ask WHAT the stream did wrong — was it its size, or the way it moved?

standards.standard_description: Describe characters in a story and explain how their actions contribute to the sequence of events.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'Why did the character do that? What happened because of their choice?'

questions.prompt:
Dina noticed her classmate sitting alone at lunch every day. She walked over and said, "Want to sit with us?" Soon they became best friends and started a book club together.

What character trait does Dina show?
questions.hint: Dina noticed someone alone and invited them to join. What kind of person does that?

questions.prompt:
When the team's star player got hurt, nobody wanted to take her place. Jaylen was nervous, but he stepped up and said, "I'll do it." He scored the winning goal.

How did Jaylen's action affect the story?
choices: His bravery led to the team winning
questions.correct: His bravery led to the team winning
questions.hint: Jaylen volunteered even though he was nervous. What happened because of that?

questions.prompt:
Omar found a wallet on the playground with twenty dollars inside. He thought about keeping it, but instead he brought it to the office. The wallet belonged to a kindergartner's mom.

What does Omar's action tell us about him?
choices: He is scared of getting caught
He is honest and does the right thing
questions.correct: He is honest and does the right thing
questions.hint: Omar chose to return the wallet even though he could have kept the money.

questions.prompt:
Marta refused to share the art supplies with her group. She kept the best markers for herself. The other kids stopped inviting her to work on projects.

What happened BECAUSE of Marta's actions?
questions.hint: Marta's selfishness had a consequence. What was it?

questions.prompt:
Lily wanted to build the tallest sandcastle on the beach. First, she watched how the waves came in and where the sand was firmest. Then, she drew a small picture of her castle in the sand with a stick. She decided to dig a wide, shallow moat first, so the water wouldn't wash her castle away. Only after she had a clear idea did she start piling up sand for the towers.

Based on her actions in the story, which word best describes Lily?
questions.hint: Think about how Lily prepared and planned before she started building her sandcastle.

questions.prompt:
Marisol wanted to win the science fair. When her partner Cole suggested a volcano, she said it sounded good, but that night she quietly rebuilt the whole project into a solar oven by herself. The next day she told Cole, "I fixed it so we'd win." Cole didn't say anything, but he stopped bringing his notebook to their meetings and started eating lunch with a different group.

Why did Cole most likely change how he acted?
choices: He felt pushed aside when Marisol changed the project without him
He forgot his notebook at home by accident
He thought the solar oven was too hard to build
He was excited to try a brand-new group of friends
questions.correct: He felt pushed aside when Marisol changed the project without him
questions.hint: Think about how Cole would feel after his idea was replaced overnight, then connect that feeling to the two things he stopped doing.

questions.prompt:
Every recess, Theo lined his toy cars up in the exact same order and got upset if anyone moved them. When the class got a new student, Priya, she asked to play and reached for a car. Theo pulled it back. But the next day, Theo left one red car at the edge of the mat and said to Priya, "This one can be yours."

What does Theo leaving the red car at the edge of the mat show about how he is changing?
choices: He wants Priya to line the cars up for him
He is starting to share, even though it is hard for him
He no longer cares about his cars at all
He is trying to trick Priya into leaving
questions.correct: He is starting to share, even though it is hard for him
questions.hint: Compare what Theo did on the first day with what he did on the second day, and remember how much order matters to him.

questions.prompt:
Nadia promised her little brother she would take him to the pond to catch tadpoles on Saturday. On Saturday morning, her best friend called and invited her to a pool party. Nadia looked at her brother, who was already holding the net by the door. She told her friend, "Maybe next time," and picked up her boots.

How does Nadia's choice move the story forward?
choices: Nadia stays home because she cannot decide
Nadia gives the net to her friend instead
Nadia keeps her promise, so she and her brother go to the pond
Nadia goes to the pool party and forgets her brother
questions.correct: Nadia keeps her promise, so she and her brother go to the pond
questions.hint: Notice the very last thing Nadia does before deciding, and what she says to her friend.

questions.prompt:
Big storms scared Lena. During thunder, she usually hid under her blanket. One night the power went out and her baby cousin started crying in the dark. Lena's hands were shaking, but she grabbed the flashlight, crawled to her cousin, and started singing a silly song until he laughed.

What do Lena's actions during the storm mostly reveal about her?
choices: She wanted the flashlight for herself
She is no longer scared of storms at all
She only sings when the power goes out
She can be brave for someone else even when she is afraid
questions.correct: She can be brave for someone else even when she is afraid
questions.hint: Look at what Lena feels ('hands shaking') at the same time as what she chooses to do, and who she does it for.

questions.prompt:
Omar always finished the class relay by running as fast as he could. In the big race, the runner ahead of him tripped and fell right in his path. Omar was about to pass, but he slowed down, helped the boy up, and they crossed the finish line last, together. The coach didn't give Omar a medal, but she wrote his name at the top of the 'Good Sport' board.

Why did the coach put Omar's name on the 'Good Sport' board instead of giving him a medal?
choices: Omar chose to help someone instead of winning
Omar was too tired to finish first
Omar ran faster than everyone in the race
Omar asked the coach for the board instead of a medal
questions.correct: Omar chose to help someone instead of winning
questions.hint: Think about what Omar gave up when he slowed down, and why that would matter to a coach more than speed.

questions.prompt: Maya saw a lost puppy shivering in the rain. She picked it up, wrapped it in her warm jacket, and carried it home. What kind of character is Maya?
questions.hint: Look at what Maya does for the puppy. Wrapping it up and taking it home shows she wants to help.

questions.prompt: Sam forgot to bring his homework to school. Because of this, what happened next?
choices: He could not turn in his homework
questions.correct: He could not turn in his homework
questions.hint: If you forget your homework at home, it is not with you at school. Pick the choice that makes sense.

questions.prompt: Every morning, Leo made his bed, packed his lunch, and got to the bus on time all by himself. What word best describes Leo?
questions.hint: Leo does his jobs on his own and is never late. That shows he can be trusted to do things.

questions.prompt: Priya practiced the piano a little bit every single day, even when it was hard. Because she kept practicing, what most likely happened?
choices: She got better at the piano
questions.correct: She got better at the piano
questions.hint: When you practice something every day, you usually improve. Pick the choice that shows practice paying off.

questions.prompt: When the new student looked confused, Ben smiled, waved, and said, "Come sit with me!" What kind of character is Ben?
questions.hint: Ben smiles, waves, and invites the new student over. Think about how those actions make someone feel welcome.

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of words and phrases as used in a text, distinguishing literal from nonliteral language.
standards.parent_tip: When your child reads phrases like 'raining cats and dogs,' ask 'Does that REALLY mean animals are falling from the sky?'

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In class, Maya's teacher explained that literal language means exactly what the words say, while figurative language paints a picture with words that are not meant to be taken word for word. The teacher wrote four sentences on the board and asked the students to find the one that was completely literal and could really happen just as it was written. Maya read each one carefully before raising her hand. Which sentence uses LITERAL language?
choices: Time flies when you're having fun.
She has a heart of gold.
questions.hint: Literal language means exactly what it says — no hidden meaning.

questions.prompt:
The classroom was a zoo today!

This means —
choices: They went on a field trip to the zoo
There were animals in the classroom
The classroom was very loud and chaotic
questions.correct: The classroom was very loud and chaotic
questions.hint: The classroom isn't ACTUALLY a zoo — the author is comparing it to one. Why?

standards.standard_description: Refer to parts of stories, dramas, and poems using terms such as chapter, scene, and stanza.
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child that chapters are for books, scenes for plays, and stanzas for poems.

questions.prompt: If your teacher asks you to read the second 'stanza' of a poem, what is she telling you to do?
choices: Find the second scene in a play.
Read the second group of lines in the poem.
Look for the second chapter in a book.
questions.correct: Read the second group of lines in the poem.
questions.hint: Remember that a stanza is a way poems are organized into parts.

questions.prompt: A long novel is usually divided into —
questions.hint: Think about how a novel is organized — each section has a number and sometimes a title.

questions.prompt: Read the poem below. How many stanzas are in the poem 'My Dog Spot'?My dog Spot is fluffy and white,He barks all day and sleeps all night.He loves to play with his red ball,And comes running when I call.When we go out for a walk,He likes to sniff and sometimes talk.He wags his tail with happy sound,The best dog ever, all around.He chases squirrels up a tree,And brings his favorite toy to me.He's always there, a loyal friend,Our happy times will never end.
questions.hint: Look at how the poem is arranged into different sections or groups of lines.

questions.prompt:
Read this poem.

Stanza 1:
The seed lay sleeping in the ground,
Wrapped up warm, it made no sound.

Stanza 2:
But when the spring rain came to stay,
The little seed woke up one day.

Stanza 3:
Now green and tall, it drinks the sun,
The seed's long sleepy rest is done.

How does the second stanza change the story from the first stanza?
choices: It shows the seed starting to wake up and change.
It shows a farmer planting the seed in the spring.
It shows the seed staying quiet and asleep in the ground.
It shows the seed fully grown and drinking the sun.
questions.correct: It shows the seed starting to wake up and change.
questions.hint: Each stanza is one step in the seed's life. Ask what is different in stanza 2 that was not happening yet in stanza 1.

questions.prompt:
Read this short play.

Scene 1 (In the kitchen): Mom says, "Ana, if you save ten dollars, you may get a puppy."

Scene 2 (At the park, a week later): Ana counts her coins and smiles. "I finally have enough!"

Scene 3 (At the animal shelter): Ana holds a small brown puppy. "You're coming home with me."

What most likely happened between the end of Scene 2 and the start of Scene 3, even though the play does not show it?
choices: Ana gave the ten dollars back to her mom.
Ana traveled from the park to the animal shelter.
Ana lost all of her coins at the park.
Ana decided she did not want a puppy after all.
questions.correct: Ana traveled from the park to the animal shelter.
questions.hint: A new scene usually jumps to a new place. Look at where Scene 2 ends and where Scene 3 begins, and think about what the gap skips over.

questions.prompt:
Read this poem.

Stanza 1:
The cold rain falls, the sky is gray,
and still I wait for you.

Stanza 2:
The white snow drifts and hides the day,
and still I wait for you.

Stanza 3:
The warm sun shines and clears the way,
and still I wait for you.

Why does the poet most likely end every stanza with the same line?
choices: To warn the reader that the poem is almost over.
To show that the speaker loves rain, snow, and sun.
To show that the speaker keeps waiting no matter what changes.
To show that the weather keeps changing quickly.
questions.correct: To show that the speaker keeps waiting no matter what changes.
questions.hint: The weather is different in each stanza, but one line stays the same. Ask what that unchanging line tells you about the speaker.

questions.prompt:
A chapter book has these chapter titles, in order:

Chapter 1: The Storm
Chapter 2: Alone on the Island
Chapter 3: A Sail on the Horizon

Using the order of the chapter titles, what most likely happens across the whole story?
choices: A boy watches a storm from inside his safe, dry house.
A boy sails on calm seas and never runs into any trouble.
A boy builds a boat, sells it, and then buys his own island.
A boy is caught in a storm, ends up stranded, and may finally be rescued.
questions.correct: A boy is caught in a storm, ends up stranded, and may finally be rescued.
questions.hint: Read the three titles in order like the beginning, middle, and end of one story. What does each title add to the one before it?

questions.prompt:
Read this poem.

Stanza 1:
A lonely lighthouse stands up tall,
above the waves it watches all.

Stanza 2:
The ships slip by in cloudy fog,
past rocky shore and floating log.

Stanza 3:
One ship draws near the jagged stone,
lost in the dark, afraid, alone.

Stanza 4:
The bright light turns and shows the way,
and guides the ship to safe harbor and day.

In which stanza does the poem show the lighthouse doing its most important job?
questions.hint: Think about what a lighthouse is really FOR. Find the stanza where it actually does that job, not just where it is described or where a ship is in danger.

standards.standard_description: Distinguish their own point of view from that of the narrator or those of the characters.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child 'Do you agree with what the character did? Would you have done something different?'

questions.prompt: The narrator says, "Vegetables are the most boring food in the world." How does *your* point of view about vegetables compare to the narrator's?
choices: My point of view is the same, because I also think all vegetables are boring.
My point of view is different, because I enjoy eating some vegetables.
My point of view is different, because I think vegetables are good for your body.
My point of view is different, because I don't usually eat vegetables.
questions.correct: My point of view is different, because I enjoy eating some vegetables.
questions.hint: Reread what the narrator says about vegetables and then think about your own feelings and experiences with them.

questions.prompt: A character in a story thinks it's okay to cheat on a test. You think cheating is wrong. This shows that —
choices: You and the character have different points of view
questions.correct: You and the character have different points of view
questions.hint: It's normal to disagree with a character! That's having a different point of view.

questions.prompt: Read this part of a story: 'Mia skipped happily down the path. She didn't know a small puddle was hiding just ahead!' What does the narrator tell you that Mia does not know?
choices: A small puddle is hiding just ahead.
questions.correct: A small puddle is hiding just ahead.
questions.hint: Look for what the story tells you that Mia 'didn't know'.

questions.prompt:
The wolf in the story said he was just trying to borrow sugar. But the pigs said he tried to blow their houses down.

A reader might think —
choices: The wolf is definitely telling the truth
Both characters have their own version of events, and the reader can decide
questions.correct: Both characters have their own version of events, and the reader can decide
questions.hint: As a reader, YOU get to think about both sides and form your own opinion!

standards.standard_description: Explain how specific aspects of a text's illustrations contribute to what is conveyed by the words.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'What does the picture show that the words don't? How does the picture make you feel?'

questions.prompt: A story says "The girl walked into the room." The illustration shows the room decorated with balloons, streamers, and a big cake. The illustration helps show that —
choices: There's a party or celebration happening
questions.correct: There's a party or celebration happening
questions.hint: The words just say she walked in, but the PICTURE adds all those party details!

questions.prompt: Read this story part: 'Leo crept down the long, spooky hallway. Every little creak and groan made him jump. He wondered what was hiding in the shadows.' Imagine an illustration for this story shows Leo holding a tiny, flickering flashlight, his eyes wide with worry, and tall, dark shadows stretching from corners. How does this illustration help you understand the story words?
choices: It shows that Leo is brave and not afraid at all.
It helps you see how bright and happy the hallway is.
It makes the hallway seem even more mysterious and a little scary.
It tells you exactly what Leo will find at the end of the hallway.
questions.correct: It makes the hallway seem even more mysterious and a little scary.
questions.hint: Think about what feelings the words 'spooky' and 'shadows' create, and how the picture adds to those feelings.

questions.prompt: In a picture book, the text says the character is "fine." But the illustration shows tears in the character's eyes. What does the illustration tell us?
choices: The character is lying about their feelings
questions.correct: The character is lying about their feelings
questions.hint: The words say 'fine' but the picture shows tears — those don't match!

questions.prompt: In the story, the words describe Lily: 'Lily skipped into the classroom, her backpack bouncing. She always had a big smile and loved to share her colorful drawings with everyone.' The picture shows Lily with a wide, joyful smile, her eyes sparkling, and holding up a brightly colored drawing. How does this illustration help you understand what kind of person Lily is?
choices: It shows the color of Lily's shirt.
It explains why Lily's backpack is bouncing.
It makes it clear that Lily is a cheerful and outgoing friend.
It tells you that Lily prefers drawing to reading.
questions.correct: It makes it clear that Lily is a cheerful and outgoing friend.
questions.hint: Look closely at Lily's happy expression and what she is doing with her drawing in the picture to understand her feelings and how she acts.

questions.prompt: A story describes a "small cottage." The illustration shows it surrounded by enormous trees and a vast mountain range. How does the illustration add to the story?
choices: It shows the cottage is actually big
It emphasizes how small the cottage is compared to nature
questions.correct: It emphasizes how small the cottage is compared to nature
questions.hint: The huge trees and mountains around the tiny cottage show just how small it is!

questions.prompt: The words say: "Maya put on her coat." The picture next to the words shows snow falling and a big snowman in the yard. What does the picture help you know?
choices: It is cold and snowy outside
It is a hot summer day
questions.correct: It is cold and snowy outside
questions.hint: Look at what is falling from the sky and the snowman in the picture. What kind of weather is that?

questions.prompt: The words say: "Ben looked in the box." The picture shows a fluffy puppy sitting inside the box. What does the picture help you know?
choices: There is a puppy in the box
There is a book in the box
questions.correct: There is a puppy in the box
questions.hint: The words don't say what is in the box, but the picture does. What animal is sitting inside?

questions.prompt: The words say: "The kids played outside." The picture shows the kids on a beach building a sandcastle by the water. Where are the kids playing?
choices: On a mountain covered in snow
questions.hint: Look at the sand, the water, and the sandcastle in the picture. What place has all of those?

questions.prompt: The words say: "Grandpa smiled." The picture shows Grandpa holding a birthday cake with candles and a party hat on his head. What does the picture help you know?
questions.hint: Cake, candles, and a party hat all point to one special day. What day is that?

questions.prompt: The words say: "Lily read her book." The picture shows Lily sitting in bed at night with a lamp on and the moon out the window. When is Lily reading?
questions.hint: Look at the moon out the window and the lamp turned on. What time of day is it?

standards.standard_description: Compare and contrast the themes, settings, and plots of stories written by the same author about the same or similar characters.
standards.parent_tip: If your child reads a book series, ask 'How was this adventure different from the last one?'

questions.prompt: Imagine two stories by the same author. The first story is about two friends who live near big buildings and play in a busy park. The second story is about two friends who live on a farm and play near animals in a field. Both stories show how important friendship is. Which part of the stories is clearly different?
questions.hint: Think about where the friends are living and playing in each story.

questions.prompt: In Story 1, a girl solves a mystery at her school. In Story 2 by the same author, the same girl solves a mystery at a museum. What changed between the stories?
questions.hint: Same girl, same type of story (mystery), but different place and different mystery!

questions.prompt: An author writes two stories about a character named Rosie. In the first story, Rosie learns that being kind is important. In the second story, Rosie learns that it's okay to ask for help. What literary element is different between these two stories?
choices: The type of animal Rosie meets
questions.hint: Think about what the author wants the reader to learn from each of Rosie's stories.

questions.prompt: In "The Big Race," Mira practiced alone every day and told her teammates she didn't need their help. On race day she tripped, and with no one nearby, she finished last. In "The Science Fair," Mira built her project by herself and wouldn't let her friends add any ideas. Her project fell apart on stage. Both stories are by the same author. What lesson do they BOTH teach?
choices: Trying to do everything alone can lead to trouble
Science and sports both take a lot of practice
Winning is the most important thing
You should work hard to reach your goals
questions.correct: Trying to do everything alone can lead to trouble
questions.hint: The lesson isn't spelled out. Look at what goes wrong in BOTH stories, then ask what caused it each time.

questions.prompt: Author Lin writes stories about a dog named Scout. In the first story, Scout lives on a quiet farm and spends his days napping in the sun and slowly herding sheep. In the second story, Scout moves to a busy city, where he spends his days barking at cars and dashing between crowds. By changing where Scout lives, what does the author most likely want readers to notice?
choices: Dogs should not be allowed to live in cities
A new setting can change how a character acts
Scout likes the city better than the farm
Scout is a very lazy dog
questions.correct: A new setting can change how a character acts
questions.hint: Compare what Scout DOES in each place. Then think about why the author would move him at all.

questions.prompt: The same author wrote two tales about a clever fox. In the first, the fox tricks a crow into dropping its cheese, but later a turtle tricks the fox out of a sweet berry. In the second, the fox fools a rabbit into leaving its carrot, but then a mouse fools the fox out of a nut. How are the PLOTS of the two stories alike?
choices: The fox learns to share its food with others
The fox becomes friends with the other animals
The fox tricks someone, and then gets tricked back
The fox is always the one who gets tricked
questions.correct: The fox tricks someone, and then gets tricked back
questions.hint: Follow the order of events in each story from beginning to end. Both stories have TWO tricks, not one.

questions.prompt: Kai the inventor stars in two stories by one author. In the first, Kai's flying machine crashes three times before it finally lifts into the air. In the second, Kai's robot breaks down again and again until, on the tenth try, it finally walks. The two machines are very different, yet both stories share the same big idea. What is it?
choices: Flying is more exciting than walking
Kai is not very good at inventing things
If you keep trying, you can succeed
questions.correct: If you keep trying, you can succeed
questions.hint: Ignore what the machines are. Look at the pattern of what happens each time Kai fails, and what happens in the end.

questions.prompt: An author wrote two stories about a girl named Rosa. In both stories, Rosa finds a lost pet and returns it to a worried neighbor. But in the first story she searches through a cold, snowy mountain town, and in the second she searches through a hot, dusty desert village. Which sentence BEST compares the two stories?
choices: Rosa does the same kind deed, but in a different place
The two stories have nothing in common at all
Rosa faces a completely different problem in each story
Rosa is kind in one story and unkind in the other
questions.correct: Rosa does the same kind deed, but in a different place
questions.hint: Decide two things: what stays the SAME in both stories, and what CHANGES. The best answer names both.

questions.prompt: An author wrote two stories about a boy named Max. In Story 1, Max plays soccer at the park. In Story 2, Max plays soccer at the beach. What is the SAME in both stories?
questions.hint: Look for the person who is in BOTH stories. His name is right there in each one.

questions.prompt: An author wrote two stories about a dog named Pip. In Story 1, Pip lives on a farm. In Story 2, Pip lives in a city. What is DIFFERENT between the two stories?
questions.hint: One story says farm and the other says city. Which one changed?

questions.prompt: The same author wrote two stories about a girl named Lily. Both stories teach the lesson 'be kind to others.' What do the two stories share?
questions.hint: The sentence tells you both stories teach the very same lesson. Just repeat it.

questions.prompt: An author wrote two stories about a cat named Sam. In Story 1, Sam looks for a lost toy. In Story 2, Sam looks for a lost hat. Both stories are about Sam looking for something lost. What is ALIKE in both plots?
choices: Sam is looking for something lost
questions.correct: Sam is looking for something lost
questions.hint: Both stories say Sam is looking for a lost thing. That is what they have in common.

questions.prompt: The same author wrote two stories about a boy named Theo. Story 1 happens in winter with snow. Story 2 happens in summer at the pool. What is DIFFERENT about when and where the stories happen?
questions.hint: When and where a story happens is called the setting. Snow in winter and a summer pool are not the same.

standards.standard_description: By end of the year, read and comprehend literature at the high end of the grades 2-3 text complexity band.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to tackle slightly challenging books — that's how reading muscles grow!

questions.prompt: What does the old woman's action of putting a lantern in her window every night reveal about the kind of person she is?
choices: She is afraid of the dark forest outside her home.
She is a thoughtful person who wants to help others.
She wants to see if anyone is coming to visit her.
She feels lonely and hopes someone will stop by.
questions.correct: She is a thoughtful person who wants to help others.
questions.hint: Remember why she said she put the lantern out. What does that reason tell you about her heart?

questions.prompt:
Diego had exactly one week to build his invention for the science fair. On Monday he sketched plans. By Wednesday, he'd used all his supplies and started over. On Friday, with tape on his fingers and glue in his hair, he stepped back and grinned.

What can you infer about Diego?
choices: He had help from a friend
He was persistent and creative despite setbacks
He didn't care about the science fair
questions.correct: He was persistent and creative despite setbacks
questions.hint: He started over but kept going. By Friday, he was grinning. What does that tell you?

questions.prompt: After the other kids said Sana couldn't climb the tree, what did she do next?
choices: She grabbed a branch and started to climb.
She ignored them and played with a ball.
She told them they were being mean.
questions.correct: She grabbed a branch and started to climb.
questions.hint: Look closely at what Sana does right after the other kids speak to her in the story.

questions.prompt:
The package sat on the porch for three days. Each morning, Eliot walked past it. Each evening, he wondered what was inside. On the fourth day, he finally picked it up and read the label: "To Eliot, from your future self."

What is the author trying to make the reader feel?
questions.hint: A package from your FUTURE self? The author builds mystery to make you want to know more!

questions.prompt:
Everyone said the old house was haunted. Lina didn't believe in ghosts. She marched up the creaky steps, pushed open the door, and found... a family of raccoons living in the fireplace.

What makes this ending effective?
choices: It confirms the house is haunted
It surprises the reader with a funny, unexpected twist
questions.correct: It surprises the reader with a funny, unexpected twist
questions.hint: You expected something spooky, but got raccoons! That surprise is what makes it work.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Maya carried her painting home slowly, holding it flat so the wet paint would not run. Her little brother Sam ran up the sidewalk to meet her, waving a drawing he had made all by himself. "Look what I did!" he shouted, bumping right into her arm. Maya looked down at the long smear now streaking across her sky. She opened her mouth, then closed it. "Wow, Sam," she said, kneeling to look at his drawing. "Tell me about this part right here."

Why does Maya close her mouth before she speaks?
choices: She decides not to make Sam feel bad about the accident.
She is waiting for Sam to say he is sorry first.
She cannot think of anything nice to say about Sam's drawing.
She is too tired from carrying the painting to talk.
questions.correct: She decides not to make Sam feel bad about the accident.
questions.hint: Notice what Maya was about to do (open her mouth right after seeing the smear) versus what she actually chooses to do instead.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

The whole class had voted to name the class turtle "Rocket." Only Priya had wanted "Shelly." When Mr. Lee announced the winning name, Priya clapped along with everyone and helped tape the new name card to the tank. At recess, her friend Jack asked, "Aren't you upset your name lost?" Priya shrugged. "Everybody gets a say. That's how a class works," she said, and went to line up for kickball.

What does Priya's behavior most show about her?
choices: She changed her mind and now likes the name Rocket better.
She can accept a group decision even when it isn't what she wanted.
She never really cared what the turtle was named.
She was afraid the other students would laugh at her.
questions.correct: She can accept a group decision even when it isn't what she wanted.
questions.hint: Remember that Priya DID want a different name, then look at how she acts and what she tells Jack.

questions.prompt:
Read the poem.

The old dog does not chase the ball,
He watches from the shady wall.
He knew each fence and every gate
Back when the pup was small and straight.
Now the pup runs, quick and new,
The old dog naps the afternoon through.

What is the poem mainly saying about the old dog and the pup?
choices: The pup is lost and the old dog is trying to find him.
The old dog and the pup do not like each other at all.
The pup is young and lively while the old dog is now slow with age.
The old dog is angry that the pup took his favorite ball.
questions.correct: The pup is young and lively while the old dog is now slow with age.
questions.hint: Compare the words used for the pup (quick, new) with what the old dog does now (watches, naps) across the whole poem.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Every morning, Grandpa set out two cups for tea, even though Grandma had passed away last spring. He would pour hot water into both, then sit quietly until one cup went cold. When Ravi came to visit, he noticed the second cup and started to ask about it, but Grandpa was already smiling out the window at the garden Grandma had planted. Ravi decided to pour himself some tea and sit down beside him.

Why does Grandpa most likely set out two cups each morning?
choices: He does not like to drink his tea until it has cooled off.
He forgets each day that Grandma has passed away.
He is expecting Ravi to come over for tea every morning.
He is remembering Grandma and keeping her close in his routine.
questions.correct: He is remembering Grandma and keeping her close in his routine.
questions.hint: Think about what one cup going cold, and Grandpa smiling at Grandma's garden, suggest together about his feelings.

questions.prompt:
Read the story.

Before the spelling bee, Omar had told everyone he would win easily. When he misspelled his very first word and had to sit down, he crossed his arms and muttered that the word wasn't fair. But that night, Omar quietly pulled out the word list and began writing each word three times. He taped the hardest ones to his bathroom mirror. He did not tell anyone what he was doing.

How does Omar change from the beginning of the story to the end?
choices: He stops bragging and quietly starts working to get better.
He becomes angrier at the judges as the day goes on.
He decides spelling bees are unfair and gives up on them.
He asks his friends to help him practice the word list.
questions.correct: He stops bragging and quietly starts working to get better.
questions.hint: Track Omar's actions in order: what he said at first, how he reacted after losing, and what he chose to do that night alone.

standards.standard_description: Ask and answer questions to demonstrate understanding of a text, referring explicitly to the text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child to prove their answer by finding the exact sentence in the text.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: The Amazon Rainforest is the largest rainforest on our planet, stretching across many countries in South America. Scientists have found that it produces about 20 percent of the world's oxygen, which is why some people call it the lungs of the Earth. It is also an amazing home to more than 10 million different types of plants and animals, many of which live nowhere else. Because so many living things depend on it, protecting the Amazon is very important. Which statement tells two important things about the Amazon Rainforest, according to the text?
choices: It makes about 10 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 20 million types of plants and animals.
It makes about 50 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 10 million types of plants and animals.
It makes about 20 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 10 million types of plants and animals.
It makes about 20 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 5 million types of plants and animals.
questions.correct: It makes about 20 percent of the world's oxygen and has more than 10 million types of plants and animals.
questions.hint: Look at the first and second sentences of the passage to find both facts about the Amazon Rainforest.

questions.prompt:
Harriet Tubman escaped slavery in 1849. She then risked her life many times to help others escape through the Underground Railroad. She led about 70 people to freedom.

How many people did Harriet Tubman help escape?
questions.hint: The last sentence gives you the exact number.

questions.prompt:
Earthquakes happen when large pieces of the Earth's crust, called tectonic plates, suddenly move. Most earthquakes occur along the edges of these plates.

What causes earthquakes?
questions.hint: The first sentence explains exactly what causes them.

questions.prompt: According to the text, what did Thomas Edison say he found instead of failures when trying to invent the light bulb?
questions.hint: Refer to Edison's quote in the passage to see what he said he found.

questions.prompt:
The International Space Station orbits Earth about every 90 minutes, traveling at 17,500 miles per hour. Astronauts aboard the station see 16 sunrises and sunsets every day.

Why do astronauts see 16 sunrises each day?
choices: The sun moves faster in space
The station orbits Earth so quickly
questions.correct: The station orbits Earth so quickly
questions.hint: It goes around Earth every 90 minutes — so it sees the sun rise and set with each orbit!

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Owls hunt at night. Their large eyes help them see in the dark." According to the text, when do owls hunt?
questions.hint: Look at the very first sentence. It tells you exactly when owls hunt.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "A camel can go many days without water. It stores fat in its hump." According to the text, where does a camel store fat?
questions.hint: The second sentence names the body part. Point to the word right before "hump."

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Bees make honey. They collect sweet nectar from flowers to do it." According to the text, what do bees make?
questions.hint: The first sentence says exactly what bees make. Reread it.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The Sahara is a very hot desert. It gets almost no rain all year." According to the text, how much rain does the Sahara get?
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you about the rain. Find the words that describe how much.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "Penguins live where it is cold. They use their wings to swim instead of fly." According to the text, what do penguins use their wings for?
choices: To carry food in the sky
questions.hint: The second sentence tells what penguins do with their wings. Look for the word after "to."

standards.standard_description: Determine the main idea of a text; recount the key details and explain how they support the main idea.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'What is this passage mostly about?' and 'What details help prove that main idea?'

questions.prompt:
Bees are essential to our food supply. They pollinate flowers that grow into fruits and vegetables. Without bees, we would lose about one-third of the food we eat.

What is the main idea?
choices: Bees are important for our food supply
questions.correct: Bees are important for our food supply
questions.hint: The first sentence states the main idea. The rest gives supporting details.

questions.prompt:
Coral reefs are in danger. Pollution poisons the water. Rising temperatures cause coral to turn white and die. Overfishing removes animals that keep reefs healthy.

The key details all support the main idea that —
questions.hint: Pollution, rising temperatures, overfishing — these are all threats!

questions.prompt: Read the passage below.Butterflies start as tiny eggs. The eggs hatch into larvae, which are called caterpillars. Caterpillars eat many leaves and grow bigger. After growing, a caterpillar forms a pupa around itself, often called a chrysalis. Inside the chrysalis, the caterpillar changes. Finally, a beautiful butterfly comes out!What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: Caterpillars eat many leaves to grow.
Butterflies go through different stages to grow.
A chrysalis is where a caterpillar changes.
questions.correct: Butterflies go through different stages to grow.
questions.hint: Reread the passage and think about what big idea all the sentences are teaching you.

questions.prompt:
Ancient Egyptians invented many things we still use today. They created one of the first writing systems called hieroglyphics. They developed a 365-day calendar. They even invented toothpaste!

What is the main idea?
questions.hint: The passage lists several inventions. What big idea ties them together?

questions.prompt:
Maya's class wanted a garden, but they had no money and no tools. First, they sold their old books to earn some cash. Then each student brought one small shovel or rake from home. On Saturday, teachers AND parents came to help them dig. By spring, the garden was bursting with plants.

What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: The whole class worked together, step by step, to make their garden happen
Maya's class sold their old books to earn money
Spring is the best time to grow plants
questions.correct: The whole class worked together, step by step, to make their garden happen
questions.hint: The main idea is what ALL the sentences add up to, not just one thing that happened. Ask what the whole story is really about.

questions.prompt:
Owls are built to hunt in the dark of night. Their huge eyes soak up even the tiniest bit of moonlight. The author also tells us that owls have soft, fluffy feathers.

How does the detail about soft feathers help support the main idea?
choices: Soft feathers help owls build better nests
Soft feathers let owls fly silently, so they can sneak up on prey at night
Soft feathers keep owls warm on cold nights
Soft feathers make owls look pretty and fluffy
questions.correct: Soft feathers let owls fly silently, so they can sneak up on prey at night
questions.hint: A detail supports the main idea only if it connects back to it. The main idea here is about HUNTING at night, so ask which choice ties feathers to hunting.

questions.prompt:
The Nile River gave ancient Egypt almost everything it needed. Its water let farmers grow wheat in the middle of a dry desert. When it flooded, it left behind thick mud that made rich soil for crops. Boats carried food and goods up and down the river so people could trade.

Which sentence best states the main idea?
choices: Boats can carry goods from place to place
The Nile River was very important to life in ancient Egypt
Rivers are found in many places around the world
questions.correct: The Nile River was very important to life in ancient Egypt
questions.hint: A good main idea is not too big and not too small. Cross out any choice that is way broader than the passage OR that is just one small detail from it.

questions.prompt:
Penguins have clever ways to stay warm in the freezing cold. They huddle together in huge groups so their bodies block the icy wind. They also have a thick layer of fat under their skin that holds in heat.

Which NEW sentence would best support the main idea of this passage?
choices: Baby penguins are covered in soft gray fluff
Penguins cannot fly up into the air like other birds
Penguins dive into the sea to catch fish to eat
Their feathers are packed tightly together to trap warm air against their skin
questions.correct: Their feathers are packed tightly together to trap warm air against their skin
questions.hint: The main idea is about how penguins STAY WARM. A supporting sentence must be about warmth, even if the other sentences are also true about penguins.

questions.prompt:
Many people think spiders are creepy pests they should squash. But spiders actually help us in ways we hardly notice. They catch flies and mosquitoes that carry germs. They eat the bugs that would chew holes in our garden plants. Some farmers even count on spiders to protect their crops.

What is the main idea of this passage?
choices: Spiders are actually helpful to people
Mosquitoes carry germs that can make people sick
Spiders are creepy pests that people should squash
Farmers work hard to grow their crops
questions.correct: Spiders are actually helpful to people
questions.hint: Watch the word 'But' after the first sentence. Sometimes the first line says what people WRONGLY think, and the real main idea is the opposite.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "Owls are great hunters at night. They have big eyes that see in the dark. They have sharp ears that hear tiny sounds. They have quiet wings that make no noise when they fly." What is the main idea?
choices: The moon is bright at night
questions.hint: Look at the very first sentence. It tells you what the whole paragraph is about.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "Plants need many things to grow. They need water from the rain. They need sunlight from the sky. They need good soil for their roots." What is the main idea?
choices: Plants need many things to grow
questions.correct: Plants need many things to grow
questions.hint: The first sentence tells you the big idea. The other sentences list the details.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "Dogs help people in many ways. Some dogs guide people who cannot see. Some dogs find people who are lost. Some dogs keep homes safe." What is the main idea?
choices: Cats like to nap all day
Dogs help people in many ways
questions.correct: Dogs help people in many ways
questions.hint: Which choice sums up ALL the details about what dogs do? Check the first sentence.

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "The library is a busy place. Kids read books at the tables. A teacher reads a story out loud. People use the computers to learn." What is the main idea?
choices: The library is a busy place
Books can be heavy to carry
questions.correct: The library is a busy place
questions.hint: All the details show people doing things. What one sentence covers them all?

questions.prompt: Read this paragraph: "Winter can be very cold. People wear thick coats to stay warm. Snow covers the ground. Ponds freeze into hard ice." What is the main idea?
questions.hint: The details are all about cold weather. The first sentence names the main idea.

standards.standard_description: Describe the relationship between historical events, scientific ideas, or steps in technical procedures using time, sequence, and cause/effect language.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child spot signal words like 'because,' 'as a result,' 'first,' 'then,' 'finally.'

questions.prompt:
Because the river flooded, farmers lost their crops. As a result, food prices went up that year.

What caused food prices to go up?
choices: Farmers lost crops due to flooding
questions.correct: Farmers lost crops due to flooding
questions.hint: Follow the chain: flood → lost crops → higher food prices.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: In 1955, in the city of Montgomery, a woman named Rosa Parks was riding home on a crowded bus. When the driver told her to give up her seat, she quietly refused, and she was arrested for it. The news of her brave choice spread quickly through the community. In response, thousands of people decided to stop riding the city buses altogether, and this boycott of the buses lasted for many months. What happened because Rosa Parks refused to give up her bus seat?
choices: The Supreme Court ruled bus segregation was unconstitutional.
People started a boycott of the city buses.
Many people started riding buses more often.
Rosa Parks was given a new bus seat.
questions.correct: People started a boycott of the city buses.
questions.hint: Look at what event immediately followed Rosa Parks' refusal in the passage to understand the direct relationship.

questions.prompt:
After the Great Fire of London in 1666, the city was rebuilt with stone and brick instead of wood. This made buildings much safer from future fires.

How are these two events connected?
choices: The stone buildings caused the fire
The fire caused the city to change how it built buildings
London stopped building after the fire
questions.correct: The fire caused the city to change how it built buildings
questions.hint: The fire was the cause. Rebuilding with stone was the effect.

questions.prompt:
Mangrove trees grow along tropical coastlines. Their roots extend into the water and slow down waves. Due to this, mangroves prevent erosion and protect the shore during storms.

What is the EFFECT of mangrove roots slowing down waves?
choices: The shore is protected from erosion
questions.correct: The shore is protected from erosion
questions.hint: Look for the signal phrase 'due to this' — what comes after it is the effect!

questions.prompt:
To study whale migration, scientists first attach a small tracking device to the whale. Next, they monitor the whale's location using satellites. Then, they record the data over several months. Finally, they compare routes from different years.

What do scientists do IMMEDIATELY AFTER attaching the tracking device?
choices: Monitor the whale's location using satellites
questions.correct: Monitor the whale's location using satellites
questions.hint: Look for the word 'Next' — it tells you what comes right after the first step!

questions.prompt:
Plastic breaks down into tiny pieces called microplastics. Since microplastics are so small, fish mistake them for food and eat them. Consequently, the chemicals in the plastic enter the food chain.

What causes the chemicals to enter the food chain?
questions.hint: Follow the chain: plastic becomes tiny, fish eat it, then the chemicals enter the food chain!

questions.prompt:
After a hurricane passes over the ocean, the strong winds push huge waves onto the shore. These waves carry sand, shells, and debris inland. In the following weeks, the beach slowly rebuilds as calmer waves bring sand back.

What happens FIRST in this process?
choices: A hurricane's winds push waves onto shore
questions.correct: A hurricane's winds push waves onto shore
questions.hint: Read the passage from the beginning — what event starts the whole process?

questions.prompt:
The ground got very cold. Because of the cold, the water in the pond turned to ice.

What caused the water to turn to ice?
choices: A duck landed on the pond
questions.hint: Look for the word "Because." It points right to the cause.

questions.prompt:
First, the seed was planted in the soil. Next, it was watered every day. Then, a small green plant grew.

What happened FIRST?
choices: The seed was planted in the soil
questions.correct: The seed was planted in the soil
questions.hint: Find the step that starts with the word "First."

questions.prompt:
Maria forgot her umbrella. Because of that, she got wet in the rain.

Why did Maria get wet?
choices: She jumped in a puddle on purpose
questions.hint: The words "Because of that" tell you the reason.

questions.prompt:
The volcano erupted. After the eruption, hot lava flowed down the mountain.

What happened AFTER the volcano erupted?
choices: Hot lava flowed down the mountain
questions.correct: Hot lava flowed down the mountain
questions.hint: Look for the word "After" to find what came next.

questions.prompt:
To make toast, you first put bread in the toaster. Then you push the lever down.

What do you do FIRST to make toast?
questions.hint: The word "first" shows you the very first step.

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of general academic and domain-specific words and phrases in a text.
standards.parent_tip: Help your child use context clues and word parts to figure out subject-specific vocabulary.

questions.prompt: Imagine a plant soaking up sunshine! It's also drinking water and taking in a special gas from the air. All of these things help the plant do its job of making food for itself. This important job is called photosynthesis.
choices: How plants make their own food.
A type of gas plants breathe.
questions.correct: How plants make their own food.
questions.hint: Look for the sentence that connects 'this important job' to what the plant is doing.

questions.prompt:
The Constitution is the supreme law of the United States. It establishes the structure of the government and protects citizens" rights.

What does **supreme** mean in this context?
questions.hint: The Constitution is the law that all other laws must follow — it's the top one!

questions.prompt:
Erosion is the gradual wearing away of land by water, wind, or ice. Over thousands of years, erosion carved the Grand Canyon.

What does **gradual** mean?
questions.hint: It took THOUSANDS of years. That's definitely not fast!

questions.prompt: Deer, rabbits, and cows are called herbivores. These animals eat things like grass, clover, and hay. Based on this information, what do herbivores eat?
questions.hint: Think about the food examples given for deer, rabbits, and cows.

questions.prompt:
The expedition to the South Pole took months of preparation. The team packed food, fuel, and navigation equipment for the dangerous journey.

What is an **expedition**?
choices: A long journey for a specific purpose
questions.correct: A long journey for a specific purpose
questions.hint: It took months of preparation for a dangerous journey to the South Pole.

questions.prompt:
Read this science passage:

"Salmon are strong swimmers. When it is time to lay their eggs, they must travel up the river to the place where they were born. Even when the river's current pushes hard against them, the salmon keep swimming forward and never give up."

What does the word **current** mean in this passage?
choices: The flow of water moving in a direction
The newest and most modern kind
Something that is happening right now
questions.correct: The flow of water moving in a direction
questions.hint: The word has more than one meaning. Look at what the current DOES to the salmon in the river to pick the right one.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"In winter, a bear does not die. It curls up in its cave for a long, deep sleep. Its heartbeat slows down, and it does not eat a single bite for many months. Scientists say the bear stays dormant until spring warms the air."

What does **dormant** mean here?
choices: Awake and moving around the cave
Very hungry and hunting for food
questions.hint: Each wrong answer matches ONE small detail. Find the meaning that fits the WHOLE picture the passage paints.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"The desert fox has huge, wide ears. On a blazing hot day, extra heat leaves the fox's body right through those big ears. This clever trick helps the fox keep its cool while the sun beats down on the sand."

What does **keep its cool** mean in this passage?
choices: Hide in the shade from other animals
Keep its body from getting too hot
Stay calm and not feel scared
questions.correct: Keep its body from getting too hot
questions.hint: This phrase can mean 'stay calm' in everyday talk, but check what the fox's ears are actually doing in the passage.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"Most metals are stiff. If you try to bend them too far, they will snap in two. But copper is different. You can bend a copper wire into a circle, twist it around, and straighten it out again, all without breaking it. Copper is very flexible."

What does **flexible** mean here?
choices: Quick to snap into two pieces
Hard and stiff like most metals
questions.hint: The word 'but' signals that copper is the OPPOSITE of the other metals. Use that contrast to find the meaning.

questions.prompt:
Read this passage:

"A sponge is full of tiny holes you can barely see. Set it down in a puddle of spilled milk, and the milk seems to vanish. It slips into all those little holes and stays there, so the sponge can absorb the whole spill before it drips off the table."

What does **absorb** mean in this passage?
choices: To soak up and hold inside
To make the liquid dry up in the air
To wipe the table until it shines
questions.correct: To soak up and hold inside
questions.hint: Follow where the milk goes in the passage. What happens to it because of the sponge's tiny holes?

questions.prompt:
The bakery was famous for its enormous cakes. Each one was so big that ten kids could share a single slice!

In this text, what does the word **enormous** mean?
questions.hint: The sentence says the cakes were so BIG that ten kids could share one slice. Which choice means big?

questions.prompt:
Sea turtles begin their life on a sandy beach. When the baby turtles hatch from their eggs, they crawl toward the water.

In this text, what does the word **hatch** mean?
choices: To come out of an egg
questions.correct: To come out of an egg
questions.hint: The sentence says the baby turtles hatch FROM THEIR EGGS. What are the babies doing with the eggs?

questions.prompt:
The desert is a very dry place. Almost no rain falls there, so the ground stays dusty all year long.

In this text, what does the word **dry** mean?
questions.hint: The text says almost no rain falls in the desert. What does that tell you about water?

questions.prompt:
Bees are very helpful insects. They gather sweet nectar from flowers and use it to make honey.

In this text, what does the word **gather** mean?
questions.hint: The bees GATHER nectar from flowers to make honey. What are they doing with the nectar? They are picking it up and collecting it.

questions.prompt:
A firefighter's job is dangerous. They rush into burning buildings, so they must be very brave.

In this text, what does the word **brave** mean?
questions.hint: The text says firefighters rush INTO burning buildings. Someone who does that scary job is not afraid.

standards.standard_description: Use text features and search tools to locate information relevant to a given topic efficiently.
standards.parent_tip: Practice using a table of contents, index, headings, and search bars to find information quickly.

questions.prompt: Imagine you have a science textbook and you want to quickly find all the pages that explain what volcanoes are. Which text feature would be the most helpful to use?
questions.hint: Think about which text feature lists many different topics in alphabetical order and tells you every page where that topic is mentioned in the book.

questions.prompt: You're researching dolphins online. Which search term would give you the BEST results?
questions.hint: Specific keywords help you find exactly what you need!

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading a non-fiction book about space and you want to quickly find the exact page that talks about "Mars." Which part of the book would you use to efficiently locate this specific information?
questions.hint: Look for the part of the text that explains which feature lists specific topics and their page numbers, usually found at the back of the book.

questions.prompt: Hyperlinks in a digital text allow you to —
questions.hint: Clicking a hyperlink takes you to another page or section with more information!

questions.prompt: A nonfiction book about sharks has a list at the very front that shows chapter names like "What Sharks Eat" and the page each one starts on. What is this list called?
questions.hint: It's the list at the front that names the chapters and tells you what page each one begins on.

questions.prompt: You are reading about spiders. You see a bold word you don't know: "venom." Which part of the book would tell you what "venom" means?
questions.hint: The glossary is the part of the book that gives the meanings of important words.

questions.prompt: You are typing a search on the computer because you want to learn what pandas eat. Which words should you type to get the best help?
questions.hint: Pick the words that clearly say exactly what you want to find out.

questions.prompt: In a book about the ocean, the words at the top of a section say "How Whales Breathe." What is this called, and what does it tell you?
choices: A glossary, which tells you the author
A page number, which tells you the date
A cover, which tells you the price
A heading, which tells you what that section is about
questions.correct: A heading, which tells you what that section is about
questions.hint: The bold words at the top of a section are a heading, and they tell you what you'll read about there.

questions.prompt: You want to find just the pages that talk about "rain" in a big weather book. There is a list at the back that names topics in ABC order with the pages for each one. What is that list called?
questions.hint: The list at the back in ABC order that points you to exact pages for a topic is the index.

standards.standard_description: Distinguish their own point of view from that of the author of a text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask your child 'What does the author think about this topic? Do you agree or disagree?'

questions.prompt:
After a long day at school, some kids just want to play. They might think homework is just more work. But doing a little practice at home can really help your brain remember new things. If you practice your sight words, you'll read faster. If you review your math problems, you'll feel ready for the test. Homework gives your brain a chance to grow even stronger and helps you truly understand what you learned in class.

The author of this passage most likely believes that homework is:
choices: only for when you have extra time to play.
something teachers give just to be mean.
helpful for making your brain stronger and smarter.
boring and just extra work after school.
questions.correct: helpful for making your brain stronger and smarter.
questions.hint: Look for what the author says homework *does* for your brain and learning in the passage.

questions.prompt: A student reads an article that says school should start later. The student likes waking up early. This shows —
choices: The student and author have different points of view
questions.correct: The student and author have different points of view
questions.hint: It's okay to disagree with an author! That's having your own point of view.

questions.prompt:
Read the paragraph. What is the author's point of view about dogs?

"Dogs are the best pets anyone could ask for! They are always so happy to see you when you come home, wagging their tails. Dogs love to play fetch in the park, which is great exercise for both of you. Plus, they can learn tricks like 'sit' and 'stay' very quickly. Having a dog means you always have a loyal friend by your side."
choices: Dogs mostly like to stay inside.
Dogs are sometimes hard to train.
Dogs are wonderful pets to own.
Dogs need a lot of healthy food.
questions.correct: Dogs are wonderful pets to own.
questions.hint: Look for the sentences that tell you how the author feels about dogs in the paragraph.

questions.prompt:
Zoos keep animals in small spaces where they can't roam freely. Animals deserve to live in their natural habitats.

The author most likely believes —
choices: Zoos aren't the best place for animals
questions.correct: Zoos aren't the best place for animals
questions.hint: Words like 'small spaces' and 'deserve to live in natural habitats' show the author's opinion.

questions.prompt: After reading the text, "Eating a big salad every single night is the best way to stay strong and smart. Only vegetables can give you all the vitamins you need. If you don't eat a salad, you might not be as healthy as you could be," which statement shows a different point of view you might have about staying healthy?
choices: The author is correct that everyone should eat salads every night.
Eating a big salad every night is the only way to be healthy.
Salads are good, but I think other healthy foods like fruit or milk are also important for being strong.
It doesn't matter what you eat, as long as you exercise a lot.
questions.correct: Salads are good, but I think other healthy foods like fruit or milk are also important for being strong.
questions.hint: Think about what the author says is the *only* and *best* way to be healthy, and then consider what *you* know about staying healthy.

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Every school should have a garden. Growing vegetables teaches kids patience and shows them where food really comes from. A plain, boring yard can become a place of wonder." Jordan reads this and thinks a garden sounds like too much dirty, sweaty work. What does this show?
choices: The author likes school gardens, but Jordan does not want the work
The author thinks gardens are too much work
The author and Jordan both dislike school gardens
Jordan agrees that gardens teach kids patience
questions.correct: The author likes school gardens, but Jordan does not want the work
questions.hint: Figure out how the AUTHOR feels first, then how Jordan feels. Do their feelings match?

questions.prompt: Read this part of an article about bats: "Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly. They use sounds we cannot hear to find insects at night. One little brown bat can eat thousands of bugs in a single hour. Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world." Which sentence shows the AUTHOR'S opinion, not a fact?
choices: One little brown bat can eat thousands of bugs in a single hour.
