Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world.
Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly.
They use sounds we cannot hear to find insects at night.
questions.correct: Without a doubt, bats are the most amazing animals in the whole world.
questions.hint: A fact can be checked and proven. An opinion is what the writer believes or feels. Which sentence could someone disagree with?

questions.prompt: An author writes: "Zoos are wonderful places. They let families see amazing animals up close, and they help protect animals that are in danger. Every city should build one!" Priya reads this. She agrees that zoos help protect animals, but it makes her sad to see animals living in cages. How is Priya's point of view DIFFERENT from the author's?
choices: Priya thinks every city should build a zoo
Priya and the author feel exactly the same way about zoos
Priya agrees zoos protect animals but feels they can also be sad places
Priya disagrees that zoos help protect animals
questions.correct: Priya agrees zoos protect animals but feels they can also be sad places
questions.hint: Priya agrees with ONE thing the author said and disagrees with another. Find the part where they don't match.

questions.prompt: Two kids wrote about the SAME field trip to the farm. Sam wrote: "The barn smelled terrible, my shoes got covered in mud, and it rained the whole time." Dana wrote: "We fed baby goats, picked fresh red apples, and learned how cows are milked." Even though they went on the same trip, what can you tell?
choices: Dana must have gone on a different trip than Sam
Sam and Dana felt the same way about the trip
The field trip was mostly a bad experience
The two writers had different feelings about the trip
questions.correct: The two writers had different feelings about the trip
questions.hint: Look at WHICH details each writer chose to share. What do their choices tell you about how each one felt?

questions.prompt: An author writes that video games are bad for kids because they waste time that could be spent reading books. Leo loves video games. He thinks they help him solve tricky puzzles and make friends online. If Leo wrote a note back to the author, which sentence would BEST show LEO'S own point of view?
choices: Games have taught me to solve problems and work with friends.
Kids should read more books instead of playing games.
You are right that games just waste kids' time.
Video games are bad for children.
questions.correct: Games have taught me to solve problems and work with friends.
questions.hint: Three of these sentences match what the AUTHOR believes. Pick the one that shows what LEO believes.

standards.standard_description: Use information from illustrations and the words in a text to demonstrate understanding.
standards.parent_tip: Point out maps, diagrams, and charts in nonfiction — ask what new information they provide.

questions.prompt: A science text describes the water cycle. Beside it is a diagram with arrows showing evaporation, condensation, and precipitation. The diagram helps by —
choices: Showing the process visually so it's easier to understand
questions.correct: Showing the process visually so it's easier to understand
questions.hint: Diagrams show you HOW something works in a way words alone can't.

questions.prompt: A map in a history book shows the route Lewis and Clark traveled. The map helps readers understand —
choices: How far and where they traveled
questions.correct: How far and where they traveled
questions.hint: Maps show locations and distances!

questions.prompt: A photograph in a textbook shows a volcano erupting with lava flowing down its side. The photograph adds to the text by —
choices: Showing what an eruption actually looks like
questions.correct: Showing what an eruption actually looks like
questions.hint: Photos show real-world images that help you picture what the text describes.

questions.prompt: A bar graph in a text about favorite school lunches shows pizza at 45%, tacos at 30%, and salad at 25%. What information does the graph give that the text might not?
choices: The exact percentages at a glance
questions.correct: The exact percentages at a glance
questions.hint: Graphs let you quickly SEE and COMPARE numbers!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Ben was reading a science article that described the layers of the Earth. The article explained that our planet has a hard outer crust, a thick middle layer called the mantle, and a very hot center called the core, all fitting inside one another like the layers of an onion. Ben wanted to add one picture that would help readers clearly see how these layers are arranged from the outside to the deep inside. Which illustration would BEST help a reader understand an article about the layers of the Earth?
choices: A cross-section diagram showing the crust, mantle, and core
questions.correct: A cross-section diagram showing the crust, mantle, and core
questions.hint: You can't SEE inside the Earth, so a diagram that shows a cross-section is the best way!

questions.prompt: A book about frogs has a picture of a green frog sitting on a lily pad. Below the picture are the words: "A frog rests on a leaf in the pond." What does the picture show you?
choices: A frog sitting on a leaf in the water
A dog running in a field
A bird flying in the sky
questions.correct: A frog sitting on a leaf in the water
questions.hint: Look at what is in the picture and match it to the words below it.

questions.prompt: A page about weather shows a big yellow sun with the label "Sunny Day." The text says, "On a sunny day, the sky is bright." What kind of day does the picture show?
questions.hint: The label under the picture tells you the answer in one word.

questions.prompt: A science book has a diagram of a tree. One arrow points to the bottom part and is labeled "roots." What part of the tree do the roots make?
choices: The bottom part, under the ground
questions.correct: The bottom part, under the ground
questions.hint: Follow the arrow to see which part of the tree the label "roots" points to.

questions.prompt: A book about the sun has a photo of a garden with the caption: "Plants need sunlight to grow." What does the photo and caption help you learn?
questions.hint: The caption is the sentence right under the photo. Read it to find the answer.

questions.prompt: A map in a book shows a school with a red star on it. The words say, "The red star shows where the school is." What does the red star on the map show?
questions.hint: The words tell you exactly what the red star means on the map.

standards.standard_description: Describe the logical connection between particular sentences and paragraphs in a text.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'Why did the author put this paragraph after that one? How are they connected?'

questions.prompt:
Sentence 1: Plastic bags harm ocean animals. Sentence 2: Sea turtles mistake plastic bags for jellyfish and eat them.

Sentence 2 connects to Sentence 1 by —
choices: Giving an example of how plastic harms animals
questions.correct: Giving an example of how plastic harms animals
questions.hint: Sentence 1 makes a general point. Sentence 2 gives a specific example!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: A rainbow is a curved band of colors that stretches across the sky, showing red, orange, yellow, green, blue, and purple in a bright arc. Rainbows appear when sunlight shines through tiny raindrops floating in the air. As the light passes into each drop, the drop bends it and splits it into all of its separate colors, and that is why we see the glowing arc after it rains. How do the first two paragraphs of the passage connect to each other?
choices: They compare two different kinds of natural events.
The first tells what something is, and the second tells why it happens.
They give a problem and then offer a solution.
The first gives an opinion, and the second gives facts to support it.
questions.correct: The first tells what something is, and the second tells why it happens.
questions.hint: Look at what information each paragraph provides about earthquakes.

questions.prompt:
Dogs are loyal pets. However, they require a lot of time and attention.

The word **however** shows —
questions.hint: 'However' signals that the next idea is different from or opposite to the first.

questions.prompt: A paragraph about saving water is followed by a paragraph listing specific ways to save water at home. The second paragraph —
choices: Provides specific examples to support the first paragraph
questions.correct: Provides specific examples to support the first paragraph
questions.hint: General idea → specific examples. That's a common way paragraphs connect!

questions.prompt: Read these sentences: "First, Maya watered her plants. Then, she opened the window to let in fresh air." What does the word "Then" tell us about these two things?
choices: Watering plants causes the air to be fresh.
Opening the window happened after watering the plants.
Watering plants and opening the window are opposites.
Watering plants and opening the window are both good ideas.
questions.correct: Opening the window happened after watering the plants.
questions.hint: Look at the order of the events described in the sentences and think about what 'Then' means.

questions.prompt:
Sentence 1: Rain fell hard all night. Sentence 2: Because of this, the river flooded the road.

How does Sentence 2 connect to Sentence 1?
choices: It tells what the rain caused to happen.
It asks the reader a question.
It changes the subject to something new.
It tells a funny joke about rivers.
questions.correct: It tells what the rain caused to happen.
questions.hint: The words "Because of this" tell you Sentence 2 is the result of the rain.

questions.prompt:
Sentence 1: Bees are helpful insects. Sentence 2: For example, they move pollen so flowers can grow.

How does Sentence 2 connect to Sentence 1?
choices: It lists the colors of a flower.
It gives an example that shows bees are helpful.
It says bees are not helpful at all.
It starts talking about a new animal.
questions.correct: It gives an example that shows bees are helpful.
questions.hint: The words "For example" tell you Sentence 2 is proving Sentence 1's idea.

questions.prompt:
Sentence 1: The library was too noisy for reading. Sentence 2: So the workers added a quiet room.

How does Sentence 2 connect to Sentence 1?
choices: It changes the topic to a school bus.
It tells the time the library opens.
It gives a solution to the problem in Sentence 1.
It repeats the exact same words as Sentence 1.
questions.correct: It gives a solution to the problem in Sentence 1.
questions.hint: Sentence 1 names a problem (too noisy). The word "So" tells you Sentence 2 fixes it.

questions.prompt:
Sentence 1: First, the seed was planted in the dirt. Sentence 2: Next, a tiny green plant popped up.

How does Sentence 2 connect to Sentence 1?
choices: It gives an opinion about the seed.
It talks about the weather on Mars.
It tells the next step in what happened.
questions.correct: It tells the next step in what happened.
questions.hint: The words "First" and "Next" show these sentences are steps in order.

questions.prompt:
Sentence 1: The desert is a very dry place. Sentence 2: In fact, some deserts get almost no rain for years.

How does Sentence 2 connect to Sentence 1?
choices: It adds more detail that supports Sentence 1.
It starts a story about the ocean.
It says the desert is actually very wet.
It asks what time it is.
questions.correct: It adds more detail that supports Sentence 1.
questions.hint: The words "In fact" tell you Sentence 2 backs up the idea that the desert is dry.

standards.standard_description: Compare and contrast the most important points and key details presented in two texts on the same topic.
standards.parent_tip: Read two articles on the same topic and ask your child what each one focused on differently.

questions.prompt: Text A explains how sharks find their food. Text B explains why there are fewer sharks in the ocean. How are the main ideas of Text A and Text B different?
choices: Text A is about land animals, but Text B is about sea animals.
Text A tells about how sharks get food, while Text B tells why sharks might disappear.
Text A talks about sharks swimming fast, and Text B talks about sharks sleeping.
Text A and Text B both talk about how sharks move.
questions.correct: Text A tells about how sharks get food, while Text B tells why sharks might disappear.
questions.hint: Think about what each text focuses on separately, then consider how those focuses are not the same.

questions.prompt:
Text A says: "Exercise is important because it keeps your heart healthy." Text B says: "Exercise is important because it improves mood and reduces stress."

What is DIFFERENT between the two texts?
choices: They focus on different BENEFITS of exercise
questions.correct: They focus on different BENEFITS of exercise
questions.hint: Both agree exercise is important — but they highlight different reasons!

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Priya was comparing two different articles about sharks for a class project. Her teacher reminded her that the smartest way to compare two texts is to notice which facts appear in both articles and which facts show up in only one of them. Priya sat down and thought carefully about the one question that would help her compare the texts the most. When comparing two texts, which question is MOST useful?
choices: What information is in both, and what is only in one?
questions.correct: What information is in both, and what is only in one?
questions.hint: The best comparison looks at what's shared AND what's unique to each text.

questions.prompt: Listen to the story: Text A argues that children spend far too much time in front of screens, and it says that screen time should be limited so kids stay healthy and active. Text B agrees that screens can be overused, but it also points out that educational apps and learning shows can teach children useful new skills, so it says that educational screen time can actually be helpful. Both texts talk about screens, but they reach their points in different ways. Which statement best compares the main points of Text A and Text B?
choices: Both texts agree that children should not use screens at all.
Text A and Text B both suggest that more screen time is better for learning.
Text A says screen time should be limited, but Text B says educational screen time can be helpful.
Text A focuses on playtime, while Text B focuses on outdoor activities.
questions.correct: Text A says screen time should be limited, but Text B says educational screen time can be helpful.
questions.hint: Think about the main message of Text A and the main message of Text B. How are their ideas about screen time different?

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Honeybees are helpful. When they land on a flower to drink its sweet nectar, yellow dust called pollen sticks to their fuzzy legs. They carry it to the next flower, and that helps new plants grow."

Text B: "Some farmers say honeybees do not mean to help. Bees only visit flowers to feed themselves. But while they eat, pollen rubs off onto other flowers by accident, so the crops grow better whether the bees mean to help or not."

Both texts agree that bees move pollen between flowers. What do they DISAGREE about?
choices: Whether the bees move pollen on purpose or by accident
Whether bees drink nectar from flowers
Whether bees actually touch pollen at all
Whether pollen helps new plants grow
questions.correct: Whether the bees move pollen on purpose or by accident
questions.hint: Both texts say the SAME thing happens (pollen moves). Look for the one point where the two authors would answer a question differently.

questions.prompt:
Text A: "The Sahara Desert is one of the hottest places on Earth. In the daytime the sand can get hot enough to burn your feet."

Text B: "People think deserts are always warm, but that is not true. A desert is any place that gets almost no rain. At night the Sahara can get cold enough to freeze water."

How would the AUTHOR of Text B most likely respond to Text A?
choices: "I agree, deserts are always warm places."
"That is true in the daytime, but the Sahara is not hot all the time."
"You are wrong, the Sahara is never hot."
"Deserts are hot because they get almost no rain."
questions.correct: "That is true in the daytime, but the Sahara is not hot all the time."
questions.hint: Text A only talks about daytime. Text B adds information about a different time. Which response uses BOTH facts without calling the other author wrong?

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Dogs were the first animals people tamed. Long ago, wild dogs came near camps to eat scraps. Over time, the friendliest ones stayed close to people."

Text B: "Cats mostly tamed themselves. When people first stored grain, mice came to eat it, and wild cats came to hunt the mice. People let the cats stay because they kept the mice away."

Based on BOTH texts, what is one thing that was the same about how dogs and cats first came to live with people?
choices: Both animals came to hunt mice that ate people's grain
Both animals were tamed because they were the friendliest ones
Both animals first came close because there was food near people
Both animals were caught by people and kept in cages
questions.correct: Both animals first came close because there was food near people
questions.hint: Each text gives a different reason food was there, but ask what BOTH stories share. Ignore the details that appear in only one text.

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Volcanoes can be scary, but they also help. When a volcano erupts, ash spreads over the land. After the ash breaks down, it makes the soil rich, so plants grow well near old volcanoes."

Text B: "A volcano's eruption can be very dangerous. Hot ash and rock shoot into the air, and thick ash can bury nearby farms and towns."

Both texts mention volcanic ash. Why do the two authors describe the SAME ash so differently?
choices: Only Text B is really talking about volcanoes
Text A is about a different kind of ash than Text B
Text A thinks volcanoes are safe and Text B thinks they are made up
Text A is describing what ash does long after, while Text B describes what it does right away
questions.correct: Text A is describing what ash does long after, while Text B describes what it does right away
questions.hint: The ash is the same. Look for a difference in WHEN each author is talking about — one uses the word 'after.'

questions.prompt:
Text A: "Recess is good for kids. Running and playing gives their bodies exercise, which keeps them strong and healthy."

Text B: "Recess helps kids in school. After a break to play outside, kids can pay attention better and learn more when they go back to class."

A teacher wants to argue that recess should NOT be cut to make more class time. Which text gives her the BEST reason, and why?
choices: Text B, because it shows recess actually helps kids do better at learning
Text A, because it proves kids cannot learn without exercise
Text A, because being strong and healthy is more important than school
Text B, because it says kids do not need class time at all
questions.correct: Text B, because it shows recess actually helps kids do better at learning
questions.hint: The teacher's goal is about learning and class time. Which text connects recess to school directly? Then check that the 'why' part is really what that text says.

standards.standard_description: By end of the year, read and comprehend informational texts at the high end of the grades 2-3 text complexity band.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage reading nonfiction about topics your child loves — science, animals, history, sports.

questions.prompt: According to the passage, what role do coral polyps play in the Great Barrier Reef being the largest living structure on Earth?
choices: They protect the fish living in the reef.
They are the tiny organisms that create the reef's structure.
They make the reef visible from far away.
They help the reef stretch over 1,400 miles by moving.
questions.correct: They are the tiny organisms that create the reef's structure.
questions.hint: Look at the sentence that describes what the Great Barrier Reef is made of and what it is known for globally.

questions.prompt:
Unlike most spiders, tarantulas don't spin webs to catch prey. Instead, they hunt by ambushing insects and small animals. Despite their scary appearance, most tarantulas are harmless to humans.

What is surprising about tarantulas based on this text?
choices: They are harmless despite looking scary
questions.correct: They are harmless despite looking scary
questions.hint: The word 'despite' signals something unexpected — scary-looking but harmless!

questions.prompt:
Monarch butterflies migrate up to 3,000 miles from Canada to Mexico each fall. No single butterfly makes the entire round trip — it takes multiple generations. The butterflies that fly south in fall are the great-great-grandchildren of the ones that flew north in spring.

Why is the monarch migration remarkable?
choices: Multiple generations complete what no single butterfly could
questions.correct: Multiple generations complete what no single butterfly could
questions.hint: The great-great-grandchildren finish the journey their ancestors started!

questions.prompt:
The Sahara Desert isn't just sand — only about 25% is sandy. The rest includes rocky plateaus, gravel plains, and even mountains. Temperatures can swing from over 130°F during the day to below freezing at night.

What common misconception about the Sahara does this text correct?
questions.hint: The text starts by saying the Sahara ISN'T just sand, then explains what else is there.

questions.prompt: Read the passage: "Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly. Most bats sleep during the day and hunt for food at night." When do most bats hunt for food?
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells when bats hunt. The passage says it right there.

questions.prompt: Read the passage: "A honeybee lives in a hive with thousands of other bees. The queen bee is the only bee that lays eggs." Which bee lays the eggs?
questions.hint: Look for the sentence that names which bee lays eggs. The passage points to just one.

questions.prompt: Read the passage: "The Sahara is the largest hot desert on Earth. It is so dry that very little rain falls there each year." What does the passage say about rain in the Sahara?
questions.hint: The last sentence tells you about rain in the Sahara. Read it again.

questions.prompt: Read the passage: "Sea turtles lay their eggs on sandy beaches. The mother turtle digs a hole, lays her eggs, and covers them with sand." Where do sea turtles lay their eggs?
choices: At the top of tall trees
questions.hint: Find the sentence that tells where the turtle lays her eggs. It is stated near the start.

questions.prompt: Read the passage: "Maple syrup comes from maple trees. In early spring, people collect a sweet liquid called sap from the trees to make it." What is maple syrup made from?
questions.hint: The passage tells you what people collect to make the syrup. Look for the word "sap."

standards.standard_description: Know and apply grade-level phonics and word analysis skills in decoding words.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to break unfamiliar words into familiar parts — prefixes, roots, and suffixes.

questions.prompt: What is the root word in **uncomfortable**?
questions.hint: Remove the prefix 'un-' and suffix '-able.' What's left?

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **important**?
questions.hint: Clap it out: im-por-tant. Count the claps!

questions.prompt: Which word has the same **tion** sound as **nation**?
questions.hint: Say each word. Which one ends with that 'shun' sound like 'nation'?

questions.prompt: In the word **knight,** which letters are silent?
questions.hint: Say 'knight' out loud. You hear 'nite' — which letters are you NOT hearing?

questions.prompt: Break the word **basketball** into syllables:
questions.hint: This compound-style word has three natural breaks.

questions.prompt:
In which word is **un-** a real prefix (a word part added to the front) that you can peel off to leave a smaller word?

uncle  •  until  •  untie  •  under
questions.hint: Try covering the first two letters. If a whole real word is left, un- is a true prefix; if only leftovers remain, it is just part of the word.

questions.prompt: What is the base (root) word inside **happiness**?
questions.hint: Peel off the suffix -ness, then remember that a base word ending in y often changes the y to i before the suffix is added.

questions.prompt:
Which word ends with the same **/shun/** sound as **nation**?

mountain  •  question  •  vacation  •  kitchen
questions.hint: Say each ending out loud and listen for /shun/, not /tin/ or /chun/. The -tion spelling only says /shun/ in some words.

questions.prompt: Which spelling is correct when you add **-ing** to the word **hop**?
questions.hint: A one-syllable word with one short vowel and one final consonant (like hop) doubles that consonant before -ing. Watch out for the word 'hope'.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **explain**?
questions.hint: Clap it out. Remember that a vowel team like 'ai' makes just one vowel sound, so it counts as only one syllable.

standards.standard_description: Identify and know the meaning of the most common prefixes and derivational suffixes.
standards.parent_tip: Make a prefix/suffix chart: un- = not, re- = again, -ful = full of, -less = without, -ment = act of.

questions.prompt: The prefix **dis-** in **disagree** means —
questions.hint: If you disagree, you do NOT agree.

questions.prompt: What does the suffix **-ment** mean in the word **enjoyment**?
questions.hint: Enjoyment = the act or state of enjoying!

questions.prompt: If **pre-** means **before,** what does **preview** mean?
questions.hint: Pre = before. View = see. So preview = see before!

questions.prompt: If a movie is "enjoyable," what does the suffix -able tell us about the movie?
choices: The movie made someone enjoy it.
The movie is able to be enjoyed.
The movie will be enjoyed later.
The movie has been enjoyed many times.
questions.correct: The movie is able to be enjoyed.
questions.hint: Remember that the suffix "-able" tells you if something *can* or *is able to* be done.

questions.prompt: The prefix **mis-** in **misunderstand** means —
questions.hint: If you misunderstand something, you understand it WRONGLY.

questions.prompt: In which word does **un-** work as a real prefix meaning 'not'?  **unkind** · **uncle** · **under** · **uncover**
questions.hint: A real prefix peels off and leaves a whole base word that still makes sense. Take off un- and ask if what's left flips to the opposite.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-er** can mean 'a person who does something.' In which word does **-er** mean that?  **painter** · **colder** · **faster** · **longer**
questions.hint: -er has two jobs: comparing (bigger) or naming a doer (baker). Ask which word names a person.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-less** means 'without.' Which word means 'without fear'?  **fearful** · **fearless** · **feared** · **fearing**
questions.hint: Match the meaning 'without' to the right ending: -less means without, -ful means full of.

questions.prompt: The prefix **pre-** means 'before.' What does the word **preheat** mean?  **to heat again** · **to heat before** · **to not heat** · **to heat fully**
questions.hint: Swap the prefix for its meaning, then read the base word right after it.

questions.prompt: The prefix **mis-** means 'wrongly' or 'badly.' What does the word **misspell** mean?  **to spell again** · **to spell wrongly** · **to spell before** · **to spell fully**
questions.hint: Replace mis- with its meaning and keep the base word 'spell' after it.

standards.standard_description: Decode words with common Latin suffixes.
standards.parent_tip: Practice Latin suffixes: -tion, -sion, -ible, -able — they appear in many English words!

questions.prompt: What sound does **-tion** make at the end of a word?
questions.hint: Say 'nation' — the '-tion' sounds like 'shun.'

questions.prompt: The word "active" means "full of action." Which word below uses a common Latin suffix to show that something has a lot of a certain quality?
questions.hint: Think about what each ending adds to the base word and if that ending is a common Latin suffix.

questions.prompt: The word **invention** breaks into which parts?
questions.hint: The root word is 'invent' and the suffix is '-ion.'

questions.prompt: All four words end with the /shun/ sound. In which word is that ending spelled with the letters -sion?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly and picture the ending. Three of them spell /shun/ with a t, but one uses an s.

questions.prompt: The Latin suffix -ous means 'full of' and sounds like /us/. Which word below actually ends with the suffix -ous?
questions.hint: Cover the last part of each word. Is what is left a real base word, or is /us/ just part of the whole word?

questions.prompt: The letters -tion usually say /shun/, as in 'nation.' In which word do the letters -tion say /chun/ instead?
questions.hint: Read each word out loud and listen for the one where your tongue makes a /ch/ catch right before the ending.

questions.prompt: Read these four words. Which one ends with the Latin suffix -ible?
questions.hint: You cannot hear the difference between -able and -ible, so picture each word spelled out and check the vowel before -ble.

questions.prompt: The suffix -sion can say /shun/ or /zhun/ (the soft sound in 'treasure'). In which word does -sion make the /zhun/ sound?
questions.hint: Look at the letter right before -sion. A vowel there softens the ending to /zhun/.

standards.standard_description: Decode multisyllable words.
standards.parent_tip: When your child encounters a long word, have them cover parts and decode one syllable at a time.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in **unbelievable**?
questions.hint: Clap it: un-be-liev-a-ble. That's five claps!

questions.prompt: Break **adventure** into syllables:
questions.hint: Three syllables: ad, ven, ture.

questions.prompt: Which choice correctly shows how to break the word "fantastic" into smaller parts to help you read it?
questions.hint: Remember to look for natural sound chunks, or syllables, in the word.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in **helicopter**?
questions.hint: Clap it: hel-i-cop-ter.

questions.prompt: Break **independent** into syllables:
questions.hint: Four syllables: in, de, pen, dent.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **rabbit**? Clap it out: rab-bit.
questions.hint: Clap once for each part you say: rab (clap) bit (clap).

questions.prompt: Break the word **napkin** into its two syllables.
questions.hint: Split between the two middle letters: nap + kin.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word **sunset**? Say it slowly: sun-set.
questions.hint: You hear two beats: sun-set. Count them.

questions.prompt: Break the word **basket** into its two syllables.
questions.hint: Split it in the middle: bas + ket.

questions.prompt: Which word has exactly **two** syllables?
questions.hint: Say each word and clap the parts. Muf-fin has two claps; the others have just one.

standards.standard_description: Read grade-appropriate irregularly spelled words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice tricky words like 'enough,' 'through,' 'thought' — they don't follow regular phonics rules.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the word "through" correctly?
choices: The children walked through the big gate.
I through the ball for the dog to catch.
The tiny bird flew through the deep ocean.
She wore a hat through her head.
questions.correct: The children walked through the big gate.
questions.hint: Think about what 'through' means when something moves from one side to another.

questions.prompt: Which of these words has a silent **w**?
questions.hint: One of these words has a 'w' you don't actually pronounce!

questions.prompt: Choose the correct spelling of the word that means **having knowledge**:
questions.hint: This word starts with a silent 'k' and has 'ledge' at the end.

questions.prompt:
All four of these words begin with the letters 'sw'. Which one has a SILENT letter you do not say out loud?

sword    swing    sweet    swan
questions.hint: Say each word slowly. In one of them the 'w' disappears and you jump straight to the next sound.

questions.prompt:
In the word 'listen', one of the letters is silent — you do not hear it when you say the word. Which letter is silent?

l-i-s-t-e-n
questions.hint: Say the word out loud and listen for a /t/ sound. If you can't hear it, that letter is the silent one.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence: 'I bought one ___ of new shoes.'

Which word is spelled correctly to fill the blank?
questions.hint: All the choices sound almost the same. Think about which spelling means 'two things that go together.'

questions.prompt:
The letters 'gh' can be tricky — sometimes they make a sound and sometimes they are silent. In which word do the letters 'gh' make NO sound at all?

eight    ghost    laugh    tough
questions.hint: Say each word. In some, 'gh' sounds like /g/ or /f/. Find the one where 'gh' is completely silent.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence: 'I ___ like to come and play.'

Which word is spelled correctly and has a silent 'l'?
questions.hint: The right word means 'want to' and hides a silent 'l' in the middle. Watch out for a look-alike that means part of a tree.

standards.standard_description: Read with sufficient accuracy and fluency to support comprehension.
standards.parent_tip: Have your child read aloud regularly — encourage smooth, expressive reading at a natural pace.

questions.prompt: Which choice best describes how a reader should say the sentence, "Wow, what a surprise!"
choices: In a quiet, calm voice, like telling a secret.
With a voice that shows excitement and wonder.
In a bored, flat voice, like reading a list.
With a slow, sad voice, like something is wrong.
questions.correct: With a voice that shows excitement and wonder.
questions.hint: Think about how someone would feel if they were truly surprised by something good.

questions.prompt: When a student reads a story fluently, what helps them understand the story best?
choices: Reading so fast that some words are skipped.
Stopping often to ask what every single word means.
Reading each word very slowly, one at a time.
Reading smoothly, making the words sound like talking.
questions.correct: Reading smoothly, making the words sound like talking.
questions.hint: Think about how reading smoothly helps you follow the story and know what is happening.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence below. Which word makes the sentence confusing and needs to be changed?"My sister wanted too go to the park with us."
questions.hint: Think about the different ways we use the words 'too' and 'to' and which one fits best in this sentence.

questions.prompt: How should you read this sentence? "'Watch out!" screamed the lifeguard."
questions.hint: The lifeguard SCREAMED — so read it with urgency!

questions.prompt: Imagine a student is reading the sentence 'The little bird sang a happy song.' Which choice shows the student reading every word correctly?
choices: The little bird sung a happy song.
The little bird sang a happy song.
The tiny bird sang a happy song.
The little bird sang a sad song.
questions.correct: The little bird sang a happy song.
questions.hint: To read accurately, look for the choice where every word matches the original sentence exactly, without changing any words or sounds.

questions.prompt: Which word has a SOFT g sound (the /j/ sound, like in 'giraffe')?
questions.hint: The letter g is usually soft when it comes right before an e, i, or y. Look at what letter follows each g.

questions.prompt: Which word BEGINS with a silent letter, like the k in 'knee'?
questions.hint: Some words start with two consonants where the first one is not spoken. Say each word slowly and listen for a letter you write but don't hear.

questions.prompt: How many syllables are in the word 'calculator'?
questions.hint: Clap or tap once for each vowel sound you hear: cal-cu-la-tor.

questions.prompt: In which word does the letter 'a' make the schwa sound (the lazy /uh/ sound, like in 'about')?
questions.hint: The schwa is a quiet /uh/ sound in an unstressed part of a word. Say each word and notice where the 'a' sounds like a clear short /a/ versus a mumbled /uh/.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as 'moon'?
questions.hint: Say 'moon' and stretch the middle sound. Some 'oo' words make that long sound and some make a shorter /oo/ sound. Listen for the exact match, even if it is spelled differently.

standards.standard_description: Read grade-level text with purpose and understanding.
standards.parent_tip: Before reading, set a goal: 'Let's read to find out why...' This gives reading a purpose!

questions.prompt:
The octopus is a master of disguise. It can change its color, texture, and even shape to match its surroundings in less than a second.

What makes the octopus a **master of disguise**?
choices: It can change color, texture, and shape instantly
questions.correct: It can change color, texture, and shape instantly
questions.hint: The second sentence lists all the amazing things it can change!

questions.prompt: Setting a purpose for reading means —
choices: Knowing what you want to learn or find out before you start
questions.correct: Knowing what you want to learn or find out before you start
questions.hint: Having a goal helps you focus and understand better!

questions.prompt: Based on the inventions mentioned in the text, what can you understand about Benjamin Franklin's interests?
choices: He wanted to write many books and stories.
He liked making everyday life better and safer for people.
He was interested in traveling to faraway places.
He enjoyed studying animals and plants.
questions.correct: He liked making everyday life better and safer for people.
questions.hint: Look at what each of Franklin's inventions helped people to do or what problems they solved.

questions.prompt:
Unlike most fish, seahorses swim upright and are surprisingly slow swimmers. The dwarf seahorse holds the record as the slowest fish in the ocean, moving at just 5 feet per hour.

What is unusual about how seahorses swim?
choices: They swim upright instead of horizontally
questions.correct: They swim upright instead of horizontally
questions.hint: The text says 'unlike most fish' — what do seahorses do differently?

questions.prompt: Read the story. Squeaky the squirrel was very busy. He spent his days collecting acorns and burying them near the big oak tree. He knew winter was coming soon, and he wanted to have plenty of food saved up. Squeaky worked hard to make sure he would not be hungry when the snow fell. What is the main idea of this story?
choices: Squeaky likes to play near the oak tree.
Squeaky buries food in the ground.
questions.hint: Think about what Squeaky is doing throughout the entire story and why.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SOFT g sound — the /j/ sound you hear in "gem"?
questions.hint: A soft g usually comes before e, i, or y. Say each word and listen for /j/ like in "gem" versus the hard /g/ in "go."

questions.prompt: The word "napkin" breaks into two syllables between the two middle consonants: nap-kin. Which word below breaks apart the SAME way, between two consonants?
questions.hint: Find the two consonants sitting between the vowels and split right between them. If there is only one consonant, the word usually splits after the first vowel instead.

questions.prompt: Which word has the SAME vowel sound as "field" (long e)?
questions.hint: The letters 'ie' don't always make the same sound. Say each word aloud and match its middle sound to the long e in "field."

questions.prompt: Which word has a SILENT letter — a letter you spell but do not say?
questions.hint: Say each word slowly, sound by sound. If a written letter never gets a sound, that word has a silent letter.

questions.prompt: In the word "learn," the letters 'ear' make the /er/ sound, like in "her." Which word below has that SAME /er/ sound?
questions.hint: The letters 'ear' can make three different sounds. Say each word and listen for the /er/ in "her."

standards.standard_description: Read grade-level prose and poetry orally with accuracy, appropriate rate, and expression.
standards.parent_tip: Practice reading aloud together — take turns with different characters and use expressive voices!

questions.prompt: When reading a poem aloud, you should —
choices: Pay attention to rhythm and pause at line breaks
questions.correct: Pay attention to rhythm and pause at line breaks
questions.hint: Poems have a special rhythm — reading them well means following that beat!

questions.prompt: How should you read this line? "'Please," she begged quietly, "don't go.'"
questions.hint: She 'begged quietly' — match your voice to that!

questions.prompt: Imagine you are reading a fun adventure story to your classmates. To make sure they understand and enjoy the story the most, how should you read?
choices: Read quietly, so only the people closest to you can hear.
Read at a smooth speed, making the story sound natural and easy to follow.
Read each word slowly, taking long breaks after every sentence.
Read very, very fast, trying to finish quickly.
questions.correct: Read at a smooth speed, making the story sound natural and easy to follow.
questions.hint: Think about what helps people understand a story best when you read it to them.

questions.prompt: A passage has a tense, exciting action scene. How should your reading change?
choices: Speed up slightly and add energy to your voice
questions.correct: Speed up slightly and add energy to your voice
questions.hint: Match the energy of the scene — exciting moments need exciting reading!

questions.prompt: When reading dialogue between two characters, what should you do?
choices: Use a slightly different voice for each character
Read both characters the same way
questions.correct: Use a slightly different voice for each character
questions.hint: Different voices help listeners know which character is speaking!

standards.standard_description: Use context to confirm or self-correct word recognition and understanding, rereading as necessary.
standards.parent_tip: When your child reads something that doesn't make sense, encourage them to reread and try again.

questions.prompt: You read the word **desert** as **dessert** in this sentence: "The camel walked across the hot desert." How do you know you made a mistake?
choices: Dessert doesn't make sense in a sentence about camels and heat
The sentence has too many words
questions.correct: Dessert doesn't make sense in a sentence about camels and heat
questions.hint: Camels don't walk across dessert! The context tells you the word should be 'desert.'

questions.prompt: Lily loved to watch the birds. One day, she saw a robin building its nest. It used tiny twigs and soft grass. The robin worked hard to *construct* a cozy home for its eggs. If you weren't sure what 'construct' meant, what would be the best way to understand it?
choices: Ask a grown-up what the word means.
Skip the word and keep reading the story.
Reread the sentence and think about what the robin is doing with the twigs and grass to make a home.
Try to sound out the word many times until it makes sense.
questions.correct: Reread the sentence and think about what the robin is doing with the twigs and grass to make a home.
questions.hint: Think about what the robin is doing with the twigs and grass to create its home. The surrounding words will give you clues.

questions.prompt: If you read a paragraph and it doesn't make sense, the BEST thing to do is —
choices: Move on to the next chapter
questions.hint: Slowing down and rereading helps you catch what you missed!

questions.prompt: You read **wind** as rhyming with **find** in this sentence: "The wind blew the leaves across the yard." Is that correct?
choices: You can't tell from the sentence
No, in this sentence 'wind' rhymes with 'pinned'
questions.correct: No, in this sentence 'wind' rhymes with 'pinned'
questions.hint: Context clue: wind that BLOWS is pronounced like 'pinned,' not like 'find.'

standards.standard_description: Determine or clarify the meaning of unknown and multiple-meaning words and phrases.
standards.parent_tip: Teach your child to use context clues, word parts, and dictionaries to unlock new words.

questions.prompt:
The spectators cheered wildly as the runner crossed the finish line.

What are **spectators**?
questions.hint: Spectators were cheering — they were watching the race, not running it!

questions.prompt:
You know the prefix **re-** means "again" and the word *examine* means "to look at closely." Read this sentence:

"Before turning in the test, the teacher told us to **reexamine** every answer."

What does **reexamine** mean here?
choices: Look closely at the answers again
questions.correct: Look closely at the answers again
questions.hint: Break the word into its two parts: the prefix re- and the base word examine. Put the meanings together.

questions.prompt:
Maria came inside from the snowstorm with her teeth chattering, unable to stop shivering.

Which word **best** describes how Maria felt?
questions.hint: All three cold words are correct in a general way, but the chattering teeth and shivering signal the strongest degree of cold. Rank the words from mildest to most extreme.

questions.prompt:
The Latin root **port** means "to carry." Read this sentence:

"The **porter** at the train station helped the travelers with their heavy suitcases."

Based on the root, what does a **porter** most likely do?
choices: Drives the train down the track
questions.hint: Focus on the root port, which means "to carry." Which job is mostly about carrying things?

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence:

"Dad slammed the **trunk** shut after loading the cooler and the beach chairs into the back of the car."

What does **trunk** mean in this sentence?
choices: A large box for storing clothes
The thick main stem of a tree
The storage space at the back of a car
questions.correct: The storage space at the back of a car
questions.hint: Every choice is a real meaning of "trunk." Use the clues "loading the cooler" and "back of the car" to pick the meaning that fits this sentence.

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence:

"The exhausted hikers ______ up the steep, muddy trail, dragging their heavy boots with every step."

Which word fills the blank most **precisely** to show how hard the climb was?
questions.hint: All four words could technically fit, but only one paints a vivid picture of slow, tired, heavy steps. Match the word to "exhausted" and "dragging."

questions.prompt:
Maria felt **exhausted** after running all day, so she fell asleep the moment she lay down.

What does **exhausted** mean?
questions.hint: She fell asleep right away. What do you feel when you need to sleep after a long, busy day?

questions.prompt:
The soup was **piping hot**, so Dad blew on his spoon to cool it before he ate.

What does **piping hot** mean?
questions.hint: Dad had to blow on his spoon to cool the soup down. Was the soup hot or cold?

questions.prompt:
The path was **narrow**, so only one person could walk on it at a time.

What does **narrow** mean?
questions.hint: Only one person could fit on the path at a time. Was the path wide or thin?

questions.prompt:
Read this sentence: "Please **turn** the page to read the next part of the story."

What does **turn** mean here?
choices: A player's go in a game
questions.hint: You do this to a page in a book to see the next one. You flip it!

questions.prompt:
The old bridge was **sturdy**, so the heavy truck crossed it safely without any problem.

What does **sturdy** mean?
questions.hint: A heavy truck crossed the bridge safely. Was the bridge strong or weak?

standards.standard_description: Use sentence-level context as a clue to the meaning of a word or phrase.
standards.parent_tip: When your child hits an unknown word, say 'Read the whole sentence — what clues help you figure it out?'

questions.prompt:
The nocturnal owl hunted mice while other birds slept.

Using context clues, **nocturnal** means —
questions.hint: The owl hunted while other birds SLEPT. When do birds sleep? At night!

questions.prompt:
The soup was so hot it was scalding, and it burned my tongue.

Using context clues, **scalding** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the soup burned my tongue. What kind of hot burns you?

questions.prompt:
The tired hikers rested because they were exhausted after the long walk.

Using context clues, **exhausted** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the hikers were tired and rested. What word means the same as tired?

questions.prompt:
The gigantic elephant was so big it could not fit through the door.

Using context clues, **gigantic** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the elephant was so big it could not fit. What does that tell you about gigantic?

questions.prompt:
Maria was thrilled and jumped for joy when she won the race.

Using context clues, **thrilled** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says Maria jumped for joy. How do you feel when you jump for joy?

questions.prompt:
The old bridge was fragile, so it broke easily when the truck drove over it.

Using context clues, **fragile** means —
questions.hint: The sentence says the bridge broke easily. What does fragile tell you about the bridge?

standards.standard_description: Determine the meaning of the new word formed when a known affix is added to a known word.
standards.parent_tip: Practice: 'If agree means to think the same, what does disagree mean? What about agreeable?'

questions.prompt: The word **impossible** means —
questions.hint: 'Im-' (like 'in-') means not. Impossible = not possible.

questions.prompt: After the storm knocked it down, the town had to reconstruct the old pier board by board. In this sentence, reconstruct means to —
questions.hint: Break the word into its prefix and root: re- + construct. What does 'construct' mean, and what does re- add to it?

questions.prompt: Grandpa's directions were so ______ that we drove in circles and got lost twice. Which word fits the blank BEST?
questions.hint: Two choices are built from 'help' but mean different things: one describes directions that don't help, the other describes a person who can't take care of themselves. Which one matches directions?

questions.prompt: "You misjudged how far the puddle was, so you landed right in it," Mom said. Misjudged means that you judged the distance —
questions.hint: The prefix mis- shows up in 'mistake' and 'misspell.' What does mis- do to the word it joins?

questions.prompt: Grandma held up the old locket and whispered, "This necklace is priceless." In this sentence, priceless means the necklace is —
choices: worth so much you can't put a price on it
questions.correct: worth so much you can't put a price on it
questions.hint: The suffix -less usually means 'without.' But think about WHY something would have no price — is it because it's worthless, or because it's too valuable to price?

questions.prompt: Both words are built from 'view.' Which word means to look at a movie BEFORE it is shown to everyone else?
questions.hint: One prefix means 'before' and another means 'again.' Which prefix matches 'before it is shown to everyone'?

questions.prompt: The prefix **re-** means "again." So the word **replay** means to —
questions.hint: "Re-" means "again," so add that to "play."

questions.prompt: The prefix **un-** means "not." So the word **unhappy** means —
questions.hint: "Un-" means "not," so put that in front of "happy."

questions.prompt: The suffix **-ful** means "full of." So the word **helpful** means —
questions.hint: "-ful" means "full of," so it is full of help.

questions.prompt: The prefix **un-** means "not." So the word **unlock** means to —
questions.hint: "Un-" means "not," so "unlock" is the opposite of lock.

questions.prompt: The suffix **-less** means "without." So the word **fearless** means —
questions.hint: "-less" means "without," so it means without fear.

standards.standard_description: Use a known root word as a clue to the meaning of an unknown word with the same root.
standards.parent_tip: Show your child how 'sign' connects to 'signal,' 'signature,' and 'design.'

questions.prompt: You know **graph** means "to write or draw." Which word is related to writing or drawing?
questions.hint: Photo = light. Graph = write/draw. A photograph is written with light!

questions.prompt: You know the root **port** means "to carry." Read this sentence: "The campers brought a portable stove they could carry from site to site." Using the root, what does **portable** most likely mean?
questions.hint: The sentence talks about cooking, but the ROOT tells you the real meaning. Find the part of the word that means "to carry."

questions.prompt: The root **aud** means "to hear." Read: "Hundreds of people packed the auditorium for the concert." Using the root, an **auditorium** is a place built mainly for people to —
questions.hint: Ignore what a big room COULD be used for. Find the root "aud" and let its meaning point you to the answer.

questions.prompt: The root **dict** means "to speak or say." Which word is built from this root and names a person who tells others what to do by giving spoken commands?
questions.hint: First check which words actually CONTAIN the root "dict." A word can sound bossy but not have the root at all.

questions.prompt: You know **struct** means "to build." Read: "The instructions showed us how to put the bookshelf together." The word **instructions** shares this root, so instructions most likely means information that helps you —
choices: find your way to a place
questions.hint: Locate the root "struct" inside the word and match its "build" meaning to the choices.

questions.prompt: The root **spect** means "to look or watch." Read: "Grandpa put on his spectacles so he could read the tiny print." Using the root and the sentence, **spectacles** are —
questions.hint: The root "spect" is about the eyes, not the ears. Match it to the reading clue in the sentence.

questions.prompt: You know that **bake** means "to cook in an oven." Which word uses the root **bake**?
questions.hint: Look for the word that has "bake" hiding inside it.

questions.prompt: You know that **play** means "to have fun with a game." Which word uses the root **play**?
questions.hint: Find the word that begins with "play."

questions.prompt: You know that **help** means "to give aid." Which word uses the root **help**?
questions.hint: Which word do you see the word "help" inside of?

questions.prompt: You know that **farm** means "land where crops grow." Which word uses the root **farm**?
questions.hint: Look for "farm" at the start of the word.

questions.prompt: You know that **paint** means "to put color on something." Which word uses the root **paint**?
questions.hint: Find the word that has "paint" inside it.

standards.standard_description: Use glossaries or beginning dictionaries to determine or clarify the precise meaning of key words.
standards.parent_tip: Practice dictionary skills — guide words, alphabetical order, multiple definitions.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary entry for **bark** shows two definitions: 1. The outer covering of a tree. 2. The sound a dog makes. How do you know which definition fits?

"The bark of the old oak tree was rough and gray."
questions.hint: It's describing a tree, so it's the outer covering — definition 1!

questions.prompt:
A dictionary entry for **current** lists these meanings:
1. happening right now
2. a steady flow of water or air
3. a flow of electric charge
4. the newest or most up-to-date thing

Sentence: "The lifeguard warned us that a strong current was pulling swimmers away from the shore." Which meaning fits this sentence?
choices: A steady flow of water or air
The newest or most up-to-date thing
questions.correct: A steady flow of water or air
questions.hint: Ignore the meaning of the word you hear most often. Reread the whole sentence and ask what is actually pulling the swimmers.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary entry for **wind** shows two pronunciations:
- **wind** (rhymes with 'pinned'): moving air outdoors
- **wind** (rhymes with 'find'): to turn or tighten something by twisting it

Sentence: "Grandpa has to wind his old watch every morning so it keeps the right time." Which meaning fits?
choices: To lose your breath after running
To turn or tighten something by twisting it
To follow a curving path, the way a river bends
questions.correct: To turn or tighten something by twisting it
questions.hint: Picture exactly what Grandpa does to the watch with his fingers, then match that action to a definition.

questions.prompt: A dictionary page has the guide words **trap** and **tray** printed at the top. Which one of these words would you find on that page?
questions.hint: Guide words show the first and last words on the page. Compare each choice letter by letter until you find one that lands between them.

questions.prompt:
A science glossary gives these entries:
- **transparent**: clear; light passes straight through so you can see objects on the other side
- **translucent**: lets some light through, but you cannot see clear shapes
- **opaque**: lets no light through at all

Sentence: "The frosted glass in the front door is ___: sunlight glows softly through it, but you cannot tell who is standing outside." Which word belongs in the blank?
questions.hint: Read the glossary carefully and match it to the two clues: light DOES get through, but you CANNOT see shapes.

questions.prompt:
A dictionary entry for **point** lists these meanings:
1. the sharp end of something
2. the main idea a person is trying to make
3. a unit used to keep score in a game
4. a particular moment or spot in time

Sentence: "With three seconds left on the clock, Maya sank the basket that gave her team the winning point." Which meaning fits?
choices: A unit used to keep score in a game
The main idea a person is trying to make
A particular moment or spot in time
questions.correct: A unit used to keep score in a game
questions.hint: The sentence mentions a clock and a last-second moment, but focus on what 'winning point' means in a basketball game.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: **puppy** (noun): a young dog. What does the word "puppy" mean?
questions.hint: Read the dictionary meaning right after the word. It tells you exactly what "puppy" means.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: **damp** (adjective): a little bit wet. If a towel is damp, what is it?
questions.hint: The dictionary already gives the meaning. "Damp" means a little bit wet.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: **gigantic** (adjective): very, very big. Which word means the same as "gigantic"?
questions.hint: The dictionary says "gigantic" means very, very big. Which choice also means very big?

questions.prompt: A glossary in a science book shows: **stem** (noun): the part of a plant that holds up the leaves and flowers. What does the stem do?
choices: Holds up the leaves and flowers
questions.correct: Holds up the leaves and flowers
questions.hint: The glossary meaning is written right there. It tells you what the stem does.

questions.prompt: A dictionary shows: **whisper** (verb): to talk in a very soft, quiet voice. If you whisper, how are you talking?
questions.hint: Look at the dictionary meaning. To "whisper" is to talk soft and quiet.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate understanding of figurative language, word relationships, and nuances in word meanings.
standards.parent_tip: Point out figurative language in everyday life: 'It's raining cats and dogs!' means it's raining very hard.

questions.prompt:
She had a heart of gold.

This means —
choices: She was very kind and generous
Her heart was made of gold metal
questions.correct: She was very kind and generous
questions.hint: This is figurative language — a 'heart of gold' means a person who is very kind.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a simile?
choices: She was as fast as a rocket.
questions.correct: She was as fast as a rocket.
questions.hint: A simile compares two things using 'as' or 'like.'

questions.prompt: What does **break a leg** mean?
questions.hint: This is an idiom — it means the opposite of what it sounds like! Performers say it for luck.

questions.prompt: Which two words are antonyms (opposites)?
questions.hint: Antonyms are opposites. Which pair shows opposite meanings?

questions.prompt:
The thunder grumbled like an angry old man.

This is an example of —
questions.hint: Thunder 'grumbling' is personification (human action). 'Like an angry old man' is a simile!

standards.standard_description: Distinguish the literal and nonliteral meanings of words and phrases in context.
standards.parent_tip: Ask 'Does this sentence mean exactly what it says, or is it saying something in a creative way?'

questions.prompt:
Time flies when you're having fun.

Is this literal or nonliteral?
choices: Nonliteral — it means time seems to pass quickly
questions.correct: Nonliteral — it means time seems to pass quickly
questions.hint: Time doesn't actually fly! It's a figurative way of saying time SEEMS to go fast.

questions.prompt: Which sentence is LITERAL (means exactly what it says)?
choices: The cat sat on the mat.
She has butterflies in her stomach.
questions.correct: The cat sat on the mat.
questions.hint: Only one of these sentences describes something that could actually, physically happen.

questions.prompt:
My backpack weighs a ton.

What does this really mean?
choices: The backpack is made of metal
questions.hint: A backpack can't really weigh a ton! This is an exaggeration (hyperbole).

questions.prompt:
She was drowning in homework.

This means —
choices: She fell into a pool of papers
She had way too much homework
questions.correct: She had way too much homework
questions.hint: You can't literally drown in homework! It means she had an overwhelming amount.

questions.prompt:
His words cut like a knife.

This sentence means his words were —
questions.hint: Words can't actually cut, but they CAN hurt feelings — like a knife hurts physically.

questions.prompt:
When Dad heard the bad news, his heart sank.

What does "his heart sank" MOST precisely mean here?
choices: He suddenly felt very sad and discouraged
He became very angry and shouted
He felt just a little worried
He had a problem with his heart
questions.correct: He suddenly felt very sad and discouraged
questions.hint: The phrase isn't about his real heart. Match how STRONG the feeling is to 'bad news' — not too mild, not the wrong feeling.

questions.prompt: In which sentence is "hit the roof" used NONLITERALLY (not meaning exactly what the words say)?
choices: Rain hit the roof loudly all night long.
When she saw the huge mess, Mom hit the roof.
The baseball flew up and hit the roof of the shed.
The workers hit the roof with their hammers to fix it.
questions.correct: When she saw the huge mess, Mom hit the roof.
questions.hint: Three sentences describe something really touching a roof. One uses the phrase to describe a feeling instead.

questions.prompt:
Grandpa called the new no-phones-at-dinner rule "a blessing in disguise." At first everyone complained, but soon the family talked and laughed more at dinner.

What does "a blessing in disguise" mean?
choices: A rule that nobody in the family likes
A special costume worn to a party
Something that seems bad at first but turns out to be good
A gift that is wrapped up so you can't see it
questions.correct: Something that seems bad at first but turns out to be good
questions.hint: Use the story clue: it started with complaints but ended with something good. Don't get stuck on the everyday meanings of 'blessing' or 'disguise.'

questions.prompt: Which phrase means the SAME as "let the cat out of the bag"?
questions.hint: Think about what really happens when a secret 'gets out.' Two choices sound close because telling a secret can feel like a mistake or like setting something loose.

questions.prompt:
The coach told the team, "Keep your eyes peeled for a good pass."

What does "keep your eyes peeled" mean?
choices: Close your eyes and rest them
Squint to see something far away
questions.hint: A coach wants players paying attention during a game. The word 'peeled' is used in a figurative way, not the way you peel fruit.

standards.standard_description: Identify real-life connections between words and their use.
standards.parent_tip: Connect vocabulary to real experiences: 'When have YOU felt courageous? What made it brave?'

questions.prompt: When would you describe someone as **generous**?
choices: When they keep everything for themselves
When they share with others willingly
questions.correct: When they share with others willingly
questions.hint: Generous means giving freely. Think of someone who shares!

questions.prompt: Which situation shows **cooperation**?
choices: A student working alone on a test
A group building a fort together
questions.correct: A group building a fort together
questions.hint: Cooperation means working together. Which choice shows teamwork?

questions.prompt: Which word BEST describes how you feel when you try something new and scary?
questions.hint: Trying something new and scary can make you feel nervous or anxious.

questions.prompt: A person who always tells the truth can be described as —
questions.hint: Someone truthful is honest — a word connected to trust.

questions.prompt: Which word describes a person who keeps trying even when things are difficult?
questions.hint: Think of someone who doesn't give up. They per-sist!

questions.prompt: When would you call someone **kind**?
choices: When they help a friend who is hurt
When they are taking a nap
When they are wearing a blue shirt
questions.correct: When they help a friend who is hurt
questions.hint: Think about what a kind person does for others. Which one is a nice, caring thing to do?

questions.prompt: Which of these is something **brave**?
choices: Speaking up in front of the whole class
questions.correct: Speaking up in front of the whole class
questions.hint: Brave means doing something even when it feels scary. Which choice takes courage?

questions.prompt: When would you say a person is **hungry**?
choices: When they turn on a lamp
When their stomach is growling and they want food
questions.correct: When their stomach is growling and they want food
questions.hint: Hungry is how you feel when your body needs food. Which choice is about wanting to eat?

questions.prompt: Which one shows a **messy** room?
choices: A room where everything is put away neatly
A room with an empty desk
Toys and clothes all over the floor
questions.correct: Toys and clothes all over the floor
questions.hint: Messy means not clean or not put away. Which room looks like a big jumble?

questions.prompt: When would you feel **tired**?
choices: After staying up all night long
questions.correct: After staying up all night long
questions.hint: Tired is how you feel when you need to rest or sleep. Which choice would wear you out?

standards.standard_description: Distinguish shades of meaning among related words that describe states of mind or degrees of certainty.
standards.parent_tip: Play 'word ladders' — arrange words by intensity: annoyed → angry → furious.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the STRONGEST feeling of anger?
questions.hint: All these words relate to anger, but which one is the most intense?

questions.prompt: Which word means MORE than **happy** but LESS than **ecstatic**?
questions.hint: Happy → delighted → ecstatic. Delighted is in the middle!

questions.prompt:
I heard the rumor about the snow day." vs. "I knew school would be canceled.

Which shows MORE certainty?
questions.hint: Hearing something is secondhand information. Knowing is being sure!

questions.prompt: Read the sentences. Which one shows that Dad is LEAST sure it will rain?
questions.hint: Line the words up from most sure to least sure: 'definitely/sure' are top, then 'probably,' then the weakest one.

questions.prompt: Which word shows MORE certainty than **maybe** but LESS certainty than **definitely**?
questions.hint: Two of these mean almost the same as 'maybe' or 'definitely' — you need the one that sits in the middle of the ladder.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the STRONGEST feeling of fear?
questions.hint: Picture the feelings from a tiny worry up to complete panic, and pick the one at the very top.

questions.prompt: Which word describes the DEEPEST sadness?
questions.hint: Rank them from a little bit sad to totally sad, then choose the heaviest one.

questions.prompt: Maria said, "I'm POSITIVE the library is open today." In this sentence, **positive** means —
questions.hint: 'Positive' has several meanings — use the rest of Maria's sentence to decide which one fits here.

questions.prompt: Which word means a BIGGER feeling of happy than just "glad"?
questions.hint: Look for the word that is still about being happy, only stronger. Three of the choices are not about happy at all.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the STRONGEST fear?
questions.hint: Only one choice is a fear word. Pick the one that means very, very scared.

questions.prompt: Sam was SURE he locked the door. Which word means Sam felt SURE?
questions.hint: "Sure" and "certain" mean the same thing. The other words do not tell how sure you feel.

questions.prompt: Which word shows the STRONGEST anger?
questions.hint: Look for the anger word. Furious means way past just a little mad.

questions.prompt: Maya was NOT sure it would rain. Which word means Maya felt NOT sure?
questions.hint: "Not sure" is another way to say "unsure." The other words do not tell how sure she felt.

standards.standard_description: Acquire and use accurately conversational, general academic, and domain-specific words and phrases.
standards.parent_tip: Encourage your child to use precise words: instead of 'nice,' try 'generous,' 'thoughtful,' or 'delightful.'

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses more precise vocabulary?
choices: The pasta was delicious and perfectly seasoned.
questions.correct: The pasta was delicious and perfectly seasoned.
questions.hint: Precise words paint a clearer picture. Which sentence tells you the MOST?

questions.prompt: In science, **habitat** means —
choices: The natural home of an animal or plant
questions.correct: The natural home of an animal or plant
questions.hint: This is a domain-specific word used in science to describe where organisms live.

questions.prompt: Pick the best word to replace 'said' in this sentence.
questions.hint: She can't believe it! That shows surprise and excitement.

questions.prompt: The academic word **compare** means to —
choices: Find how things are alike and different
questions.correct: Find how things are alike and different
questions.hint: 'Compare' is a word you use in all subjects — it means looking at similarities and differences.

questions.prompt: Read the sentence: "The tired dog wanted to rest." What does the word **tired** mean?
questions.hint: The sentence says the dog wanted to rest. What kind of dog wants to rest?

questions.prompt: In science class, an animal's **home** is the place where it lives. Which word means the same as **home**?
questions.hint: A home is a place. What does the sentence tell you a home is?

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses a more exact, describing word?
choices: The juicy, red apple looked sweet.
questions.correct: The juicy, red apple looked sweet.
questions.hint: Which sentence gives you clear details you can picture, like color and taste?

questions.prompt: Read: "The huge elephant was the biggest animal at the zoo." What does **huge** mean?
questions.hint: The sentence says the elephant was the biggest animal. So huge means very...?

questions.prompt: In a story, someone who feels **happy** feels —
questions.hint: Think about how you feel when something good happens. That is what happy means.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English grammar and usage when writing or speaking
standards.parent_tip: Verb tenses, subject-verb agreement, comparatives.

questions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a verb to show something happened in the past?
choices: My little brother run fast at the race.
My little brother running fast at the race.
My little brother ran fast at the race.
My little brother runs fast at the race.
questions.correct: My little brother ran fast at the race.
questions.hint: Remember that some action words change completely when you talk about something that already happened.

questions.prompt: Which sentence agrees correctly?
questions.hint: Dogs is plural — use run, not runs.

questions.prompt: What is the comparative form of **fast**?
questions.hint: Add -er to compare two things!

questions.prompt: Which is the superlative form of **happy**?
questions.hint: Use -est when comparing three or more.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses the right verb tense?
choices: I am eating an apple now.
I were eating an apple now.
I am eat an apple now.
I am ate an apple now.
questions.correct: I am eating an apple now.
questions.hint: Now means present. Use am eating!

questions.prompt: Ben ran faster than Sara. Tom ran faster than Ben. Which sentence is correct?
questions.hint: Line up who beat whom to find the single fastest runner, then pick the form used when comparing three or more.

questions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses the correct past-tense verbs: 'Yesterday our class ___ to the museum and ___ many old paintings.'
questions.hint: Both blanks need the plain past-tense form that stands alone without a helper word like 'has' or 'had.'

questions.prompt: Which word best completes the sentence to show WHY it happened? '___ she practiced every day, Mia became a wonderful violin player.'
questions.hint: You need a joining word that can start the sentence and signals a reason or cause.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct word: 'Of everyone in the room, Jonah spoke ___, so no one could hear him at all.'
questions.hint: You are comparing Jonah to a whole group, and the word describes HOW he spoke, so it needs the -ly adverb form.

questions.prompt: Which sentence keeps the verb tenses correct? 'Last summer we ___ to the lake, and next summer we ___ there again.'
questions.hint: Match each verb to its time clue: 'last summer' already happened, 'next summer' has not happened yet.

standards.standard_description: Demonstrate command of the conventions of standard English capitalization, punctuation, and spelling
standards.parent_tip: Quotation marks for talking, commas for lists and dates.

questions.prompt: Which uses quotation marks correctly?
questions.hint: Wrap the spoken words in quotation marks!

questions.prompt: What punctuation is needed in a date like **March 5 2026**?
choices: a comma between day and year
questions.correct: a comma between day and year
questions.hint: Always put a comma between the day and year!

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?
choices: I bought apples, bananas, and grapes.
I bought apples bananas and grapes.
I bought, apples, bananas, and, grapes.
I bought apples; bananas; and grapes.
questions.correct: I bought apples, bananas, and grapes.
questions.hint: Use commas to separate items in a list!

questions.prompt: Which is **possessive** (shows owning)?
questions.hint: Apostrophe + S shows owning!

questions.prompt: Where do quotation marks go in: She said hello?
questions.hint: Spoken words go inside quotation marks!

questions.prompt: Which sentence is punctuated correctly?
questions.hint: When a quote ends with a strong mark like ! , that mark stays inside the quotation marks and no extra comma is added before the speaker tag.

questions.prompt: Choose the correct word: "The three ___ tails wagged happily."
questions.hint: There is more than one dog, and the tails belong to them. For a plural word that already ends in -s, add just an apostrophe after the s.

questions.prompt: Choose the word that correctly fills the blank: "The kitten curled up and licked ___ soft paws."
questions.hint: Try reading it as "it is" — if that doesn't make sense, you need the possessive form, which has no apostrophe.

questions.prompt: Which spelling correctly adds -ed to the word 'hop' in this sentence: "The frog ___ across the pond."
questions.hint: For a short-vowel word ending in one consonant, double the final consonant before adding -ed.

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses capital letters correctly?
choices: We visited Lake Michigan in July.
We Visited Lake Michigan in July.
We visited lake Michigan in july.
we visited Lake michigan in July.
questions.correct: We visited Lake Michigan in July.
questions.hint: Capitalize the specific name of a place and the name of a month, but not ordinary verbs like 'visited.'

standards.standard_description: Use knowledge of language and its conventions when writing, speaking, reading, or listening
standards.parent_tip: Pick words that match your audience and purpose.

questions.prompt: Which is the strongest way to say **good**?
questions.hint: Fantastic means really, really good!

questions.prompt: You are writing to a friend. Which is best?
questions.hint: Friends use casual greetings!

questions.prompt: Which word adds the most picture to **walked**?
questions.hint: Strolled means walked in a relaxed way!

questions.prompt: Which sentence has the strongest verb?
choices: The cat pounced on the toy.
The cat went on the toy.
The cat sat on the toy.
The cat was on the toy.
questions.correct: The cat pounced on the toy.
questions.hint: Pounced shows action vividly!

questions.prompt: A storm tore the roof clean off the barn. Which word best replaces "strong" in "The wind was strong" to show HOW powerful it really was?
questions.hint: Match the word to the clue in the sentence — the wind ripped a roof off. Which word carries that much force?

questions.prompt: You are writing a thank-you note to your school principal. Which sentence fits the situation best?
choices: The trip was lit, no cap.
Thank you for organizing our wonderful field trip.
Thanks a ton for the awesome trip!
questions.correct: Thank you for organizing our wonderful field trip.
questions.hint: Think about your audience. Would you talk to a principal the same way you text a friend?

questions.prompt: Marco had not eaten all day and was starving. Which word best completes the sentence for effect: "He ___ his sandwich in seconds."?
questions.hint: The most precise word should MATCH how hungry Marco was, not fight against it.

questions.prompt: Four words all describe being happy. Which one shows the SMALLEST, mildest amount of happiness?
questions.hint: Picture the words on a ladder from a little happy to bursting with happiness. Which sits on the bottom rung?

questions.prompt: An author wants readers to feel uneasy about an old house. Which word should she choose to describe it?
questions.hint: Any of these could describe an old house — pick the one whose feeling matches the mood she wants.
